VARIABLE Statement and Numerical Expressions
Following is the VARIABLE statement in a PSDL file:
{ VARIABLE { varName ( value | { TCLVAR tclVarName } ) } … }

Here:
        varName: Specifies any valid ‘C-style’ variable name.

        value: Specifies any numeric value or a numerical expression.

        tclVarName: Specifies a Tcl variable name, where the value is obtained.

You can replace any coordinate value with a variable in PSDL by using the following syntax:
… ( varName ) …

You can replace any numerical value with a numerical expression in PSDL using the following
syntax:
( numericalExpression | varName )

The value of the replaced variable can be recalled by using the varName enclosed by parenthesis.
Ensure to define a variable before recalling it in an expression.
You can define UDA as a TCL string variable, and use it in the PSDL file, as shown in the example
below:
restoreDesign test.enc test
set UDA_M1_followpin M1_followpin
route_pg -fast -psdl_file test.psdl
# reading the vdd/gnd_net string variable inside PSDL




Last Updated in September 2025                             598                              Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing



{ REGION { COREAREA }
  { LAYER METAL1 { EXCEPTAREA { BLOCK * } } { UDA { TCLVAR UDA_M1_followpin } }
       { METAL m1_fpin_vdd m1_followpin { NET VDD } }
       { METAL m1_fpin_vss m1_followpin { NET GND } }
   }

}

Following are some other examples of the VARIABLE statement:
       Following is an example of defining a numerical expression in a PSDL file:
       { VARIABLE { x1 0.048 } { x2 1.025 } { y 0.110 } }
       { METAL met1 pat1 { NET vdd } { OFFSET ((x2) + 2 * (x1)) (y) } … }
       { METAL met2 pat2 { NET vss } { OFFSET (x2) (y) } … }

       Following is an example of defining a Tcl variable in the Tcl file and reading it inside the
       PSDL file. You can apply this Tcl variable only for a single value.
       restoreDesign test.enc test
       set abc 0.040
       route_pg –fast –psdl_file test.psdl # reading the abc variable inside PSDL
       { VARIABLE { x 0.02 } { y (0.104 * 2) } { pitch 0.560 } { a {TCLVAR abc} }
       { METAL met1 pat1 { NET vdd } { OFFSET ((x) + 2 * (a)) (y) } … }

       Following is an example of defining a Tcl variable as a list of rectangles/polygons, which can
       be used in an AREA statement:
       restoreDesign test.enc test
       set boxes_list { {61 45 89 68} {61 20 77 39} }
       route_pg –fast –psdl_file test.psdl # reading the boxes_list variable inside PSDL
       { REGION { AREA abcd { TCLVAR boxes_list } }
           { LAYER METAL1 { EXCEPTAREA { BLOCK * } }
                  { METAL m1_fpin_vdd m1_followpin { NET {TCLVAR vdd_net} } }
                  { METAL m1_fpin_vss m1_followpin { NET {TCLVAR gnd_net} } }
            } …
       }

       Following is an example of defining a Tcl variable as a list of names for
       CELL/BLOCK/INSTANCE/POWERDOMAIN, which can be used in an AREA statement:
       restoreDesign test.enc test
       set newInst { test5 test6 }
       route_pg –fast –psdl_file test.psdl                        # reading the abc variable inside PSDL




Last Updated in September 2025                             599                              Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing



       { REGION      { AREA ps_cell { INSTANCE {TCLVAR newInst } } }
       { LAYER METAL2 { SNAPTO GRID }
       { METAL m2_ps m2_staple { NET VDD } { OFFSET 2.4 0.8 } { STEPDISTANCE 2.2 2.7 } }
       } ...}

       Following is an example of defining a Tcl string variable in a Tcl file, which can be read inside
       a PSDL file:
       restoreDesign test.enc test
       set gnd_net [get_db [get_db nets -if {.is_ground == true }] .name]
       set vdd_net [dbGet [dbGet top.pgNets.name VDD -p].name]
       route_pg –fast –psdl_file test.psdl                       # reading the vdd/gnd_net string variable
       inside PSDL
       { REGION      { COREAREA }
           { LAYER METAL1 { EXCEPTAREA { BLOCK * } }
                  { METAL m1_fpin_vdd m1_followpin { NET {TCLVAR vdd_net} } }
                  { METAL m1_fpin_vss m1_followpin { NET {TCLVAR gnd_net} } }
            } …
       }



Related Topics
       route_pg

       Components of a PSDL File
       PG Structure Description Language
       PSDL Examples


PATTERN Statement

       PATTERN FOLLOWPIN Statement
       PATTERN STRIPE Statement
       PATTERN STAPLE Statement
The PATTERN statement defines the master pattern template to be used for constructing the PG
structure for each layer. You can use the same pattern multiple times for different layers, if the
constraints are the same. Following is the PATTERN statement in a PSDL file:



Last Updated in September 2025                             600                              Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing



{ PATTERN patternName
  { TYPE ( FOLLOWPIN | STRIPE | STAPLE) }
  [ { WIDTH width } ]
  [ { DIRECTION ( HORIZONTAL | VERTICAL ) } ]
  [ { WIDTH ( widthi | DEFAULT | TRACKWIDTH ) … } ]
    [ { USEFULLPATTERN } ]
    [ { SPACING ( spacingi | MINSPACING | { PITCH numPitches } ) … } ]
    [ { LENGTH     [ minLength ] ( maxLength | AUTO ) [ HARD ] } ]
}

Following are the various arguments used in the PATTERN statement:
       TYPE: Indicates the pattern type as FOLLOWPIN, STRIPE, or STAPLE.
       WIDTH: Creates a followpin on non-pin layers. To use this argument, set the TYPE argument
       to FOLLOWPIN in the PATTERN statement specifying the non-pin layers. You can also
       define the followpin pattern width using the WIDTH argument. When enabled, the adjacent
       followpin wires will automatically comply with DRC clean.
       DIRECTION: Indicates the direction of the metal as HORIZONTAL or VERTICAL If this option
       is not specified, the routing direction of the layer is used.
       WIDTH: Specifies the number of metals to be placed in the current pattern as well as the width
       for each metal in the pattern. If the DEFAULT value is specified (instead of actual width
       value), then the default metal width of the given layer is used. Similarly, non-default width is
       used if the specified NONDEFAULTRULE name is specified. The TRACKWIDTH option
       uses the track-defined width as the value of the wire width.
       USEFULLPATTERN: Allows Flash PG to accept only whole pattern for building the PG
       structure region, when it reaches the end of the region, or when there are DRC violations in its
       surrounding.. When this option is not specified, Flash PG can also accept a partial pattern.
       SPACING: Specifies the spacing required between two adjacent metals. This argument is
       optional if there is a single metal. The value of the spacing can be an absolute spacing value
       or one of the followings:
              TRACK numTracks: Specifies that the spacing is defined by the number of routing
              tracks away from the previous metal.
              PITCH numPitches: Specifies that the spacing is defined by the number of routing
              pitches away from the previous metal (measured from center-to-center).




Last Updated in September 2025                             601                              Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing




                    The PITCH spacing value is retrieved from the tech LEF file, which is the center-
                    to-center distance between two wires.

              MINSPACING: Uses minimum spacing defined in the library for the current layer based
              on the specified wire width and length. .Spacing is the closest edge measurement
              between two adjacent metals.
       LENGTH: Specifies the minimum and maximum metal length for the STAPLE type.
              When AUTO is specified, the staple length is the calculated based on the minimum area
              rule (MAR) or vias and its enclosure. This attribute is ignored for FOLLOWPIN or
              STRIPES.
              When [ HARD ] is specified, Flash PG honors user-specified staple length, with ignoring
              its DRC length violation, which can be fixed at a later stage. However, staple wire will
              be deleted when there is a DRC with an existing OBS and routing blockage.
Following is an example of the PATTERN statement where Flash PG honors user-specified staple
length:
{ PATTERN pat0 { TYPE FOLLOWPIN } }
{ PATTERN pat1 { TYPE STAPLE } { DIRECTION VERTICAL } { WIDTH 0.29 } { LENGTH 0.049
HARD } }
{ REGION { COREAREA }
    { LAYER M0
         { METAL m0_vdd pat0 { NET VDD } }
         { METAL m0_vss pat0 { NET VSS } }
    }
    { LAYER METAL2
         { METAL m2_vdd pat1 { NET VDD } { OFFSET 4.435 5.74} { STEPDISTANCE 8.550 5.74
} }
         { METAL m2_vss pat2 { NET VSS } { OFFSET 4.435 2.87} { STEPDISTANCE 8.550 5.74
} }
    }
}


      Since [HARD] is specified, METAL2 pat1 staple length is kept at 0.049, and not adjusted to
      0.060 DRC clean value.

Following is the output of the above example:

                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing




PATTERN FOLLOWPIN Statement

Following is the PATTERN FOLLOWPIN statement in a PSDL file:
{ PATTERN patternName
  { TYPE FOLLOWPIN }
  [ { WIDTH width } ]
  [ { CELL referenceCellName } ]
} # width is determined by the std-cells’ PG pin

When you specify CELL referenceCellName, the followpin metal width is assigning based on the
power/ground pin of the referenceCellName.

      CELL referenceCellName can be used only with the PATTERN FOLLOWPIN type.


       Following is an example of creating a followpin, m0_fpin, on the non-pin layers, whose width
       is determined by the PG pin width:
       { PATTERN m0_fpin { TYPE FOLLOWPIN } }
       { REGION {COREAREA}
            { LAYER METAL0
                  { METAL m0_fpin_vdd m0_fpin { NET vdd } }




Last Updated in September 2025                             603                              Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing



                  { METAL m0_fpin_vss m0_fpin { NET gnd } }
            } # m0_fpin’s width is determined by PG pin width
            { LAYER METAL1
                  { METAL m1_fpin_vdd m0_fpin { NET vdd } { FOLLOW * m0_fpin_vdd } {
       ALIGNMENT * CENTER } }
                  { METAL m1_fpin_vss m0_fpin { NET gnd } { FOLLOW * m0_fpin_vss } {
       ALIGNMENT * CENTER } }
            } # m1_fpin’s width is determined by PG pin width and its’ placement is based
       on previously define m0_fpin location
       }
       Following is the output of the above example:




       Following is an example of creating two followpins, m0_fpin and m1_fpin, on the non-pin
       layers, whose widths are specified as 0.4 and 0.2 respectively:
       { PATTERN m0_fpin { TYPE FOLLOWPIN } { WIDTH 0.4 } }
       { PATTERN m1_fpin { TYPE FOLLOWPIN } { WIDTH 0.2 } }
       { REGION {COREAREA}
            { LAYER METAL0
                  { METAL m0_fpin_vdd m0_fpin { NET vdd } }
                  { METAL m0_fpin_vss m0_fpin { NET gnd } }
            } # since WIDTH 0.4 is determined, followpin is aligned to std-cell pin.
            { LAYER METAL1
                  { METAL m1_fpin_vdd m1_fpin { NET vdd } { FOLLOW * m0_fpin_vdd } {
       ALIGNMENT * CENTER } }
                  { METAL m1_fpin_vss m1_fpin { NET gnd } { FOLLOW * m0_fpin_vss } {
       ALIGNMENT * CENTER } } }




Last Updated in September 2025                             604                              Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing



       }
       Following is the output of the above example:




       Following is an example of the PATTERN FOLLOWPIN statement in which the metal width
       will be determined by the standard cells’ PG pin:
       { PATTERN m1_followpin
           { TYPE FOLLOWPIN }
       } # width is determined by the std-cells’ PG pin

       Following is an example of the PATTERN FOLLOWPIN statement used with the CELL
       referenceCellName:
       { PATTERN m1_fpin { TYPE FOLLOWPIN } { CELL cellABC } }
              { REGION
                  { COREAREA }
                  { LAYER METAL1
                  { METAL m1_fpin_vdd m1_fpin { NET vdd }

PATTERN STRIPE Statement

Following is the PATTERN STRIPE statement in a PSDL file:
{ PATTERN patternName
  { TYPE STRIPE }
  [ { DIRECTION ( HORIZONTAL | VERTICAL ) } ]
  { WIDTH ( widthi | DEFAULT | TRACKWIDTH ) … }
    [ { SPACING      ( spacingi | MINSPACING | { TRACK numTracks }
                                   | { PITCH numPitches } ) … } ]
}


      The pitch SPACING value is retrieved from the tech LEF file, which is the center-to-center
      distance between two wires.

See the examples below:
       Following is an example of the PATTERN STRIPE statement:
       { PATTERN stripe_example
       { TYPE STRIPE }
              { DIRECTION HORIZONTAL }
              { WIDTH DEFAULT DEFAULT }
              { SPACING { PITCH 2 } }
       }
       Following is the output of the above example:

                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing

       { PATTERN stripeH1 { TYPE STRIPE } { DIRECTION HORIZONTAL } { WIDTH 0.36 } }
       Following is the output of the above example:




       Following is an example of creating a group of three horizontal global stripes with 0.36 width
       each and different spacing:
       { PATTERN stripeH2 { TYPE STRIPE } { DIRECTION HORIZONTAL } { WIDTH 0.36 0.36 0.36
       } { SPACING 0.5 0.3} }

       and same spacing:

       0.30 0.36 } { SPACING 0.5 } }

PATTERN STAPLE Statement

Following is the PATTERN STAPLE statement in a PSDL file:
{ PATTERN patternName
  { TYPE STAPLE }
  [ { DIRECTION ( HORIZONTAL | VERTICAL ) } ]
  { WIDTH ( widthi | DEFAULT | TRACKWIDTH ) … }
    [ { SPACING      ( spacingi | MINSPACING | { TRACK numTracks }
                             | { PITCH numPitches } ) … } ]
    [ { LENGTH     [ minLength ] ( maxLength | AUTO ) [ HARD ] } ]
}

In this statement:
       The PITCH SPACING value is retrieved from the tech LEF file, which is the center-to-center
       distance between two wires.
       When only maxLength is specified, both maxLength and minLength have the same value.
       When both maxLength and minLength are less than the Minimum Area Rule (MAR) value;
       MAR will be used for both minLengh and maxLength to ensure a DRC clean result.
       When only minLength is less than MAR, then MAR will be used for minLength
       LENGTH: Specifies the minimum and maximum metal length for the STAPLE type.
              When AUTO is specified, the staple length is the calculated based on the minimum area
              rule (MAR) or vias and its enclosure. This attribute is ignored for FOLLOWPIN or
              STRIPES.
              When [ HARD ] is specified, Flash PG honors user-specified staple length, with ignoring
              its DRC length violation, which can be fixed at a later stage. However, staple wire will
              be deleted when there is a DRC with an existing OBS and routing blockage.
See the examples below:
       Following is an example of the PATTERN STAPLE statement with different WIDTH,
       SPACING, and LENGTH values:
       { PATTERN staple_example
       { TYPE STAPLE }
              { DIRECTION VERTICAL }
              { WIDTH w1 w2 w3 … wn }

              { SPACING s1 s2 … sn-1 }




Last Updated in September 2025                             608                              Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing



              { LENGTH Lmin Lmax }

       }
       Following is the output of the above example:




       Following is an example of the PATTERN STAPLE statement with vertical DIRECTION,
       default WIDTH, SPACING as TRACK 2, and LENGTH as L:
       { PATTERN track_example
       { TYPE STAPLE }
              { DIRECTION VERTICAL }
              { WIDTH DEFAULT DEFAULT DEFAULT }
              { SPACING { TRACK 2 } { TRACK 2 } }
              { LENGTH L L }
       }
       Following is the output of the above example:




Last Updated in September 2025                             609                              Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing




       Following is an example of the PATTERN STAPLE statement with vertical DIRECTION,
       default WIDTH, SPACING as PITCH 2, and LENGTH as L:
       { PATTERN pitch_example
       { TYPE STAPLE }
              { DIRECTION VERTICAL }
              { WIDTH DEFAULT DEFAULT DEFAULT }
              { SPACING { PITCH 2 } { PITCH 2 } }
              { LENGTH L L }
       }
       Following is the output of the above example:

USERGRID and SNAPTO Statements

       USERGRID Statement
       SNAPTO Statement




Last Updated in September 2025                             612                              Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing



USERGRID Statement

The USERGRID statement specifies a user-defined uniform grid line for snapping PG wires to the
grid or for using as the metal pattern reference location. This grid is independent of the routing the
TRACK definition specified in the design. Following is the USERGRID statement in a PSDL file:
{ USERGRID gridName startOffset stepDistance [FROM ( CORE | DESIGN | ZERO ) ] }

Here:
        gridName: Specifies a unique identifier to represent the specified user grid.

        startOffset: Specifies the starting coordinate of the grid lines

        stepDistance: Specifies the distance between two grid lines.

        [FROM ( CORE | DESIGN | ZERO)]: Specifies the absolute starting offset coordinate value for
        the grid area. Use one of the following values:
              CORE: Sets the absolute starting offset based on the CORE bounding box lower-left
              coordinate.
              DESIGN: Sets the absolute starting offset area based on the DESIGN bounding box
              lower-left coordinate.
              ZERO: Uses 0,0 as the default absolute starting offset value.

You can also use the USERGRID statement as a reference grid in the FOLLOW statement, at the
same time with snapping to its metal track, as shown below:
{FOLLOW ( * | xRefPatternId| USERGRID gridName) ( * | yRefPatternId| USERGRID gridName)
}

See the examples below:
        Following is an example of using the USERGRID while measuring it from the CORE area:
        { USERGRID grid1 2.00 5.00 FROM CORE }
        The above example creates a snapping grid starting from 7.00 with an interval of 5.00, as
        shown in the output below:
        grid1: 7.00, 12.00, 17.00, 22.00, 27.00, 32.00, …

        Following is an example of using the FOLLOW USERGRID, where the CENTER of the
        lowest metal will be on the left/bottom grid, when ALIGNEMENT is set to LOW:
        { USERGRID grid1 0.100 1.500 } #creates grid1: 0.100, 1.600, 3.100, 4.600, 6.100,
        …
        { REGION { COREAREA }




Last Updated in September 2025                             613                              Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing



              { LAYER METAL3 { SNAPTO GRID } ### snapping all METAL3 to its track
                  { METAL m3_vdd m3_staple { NET VDD } { FOLLOW USERGRID grid1 m1_fpin_vdd}
       { ALIGNMENT CENTER CENTER } }
                  }
       }
       Following is the output of the above example:
       grid1: 0.100, 1.600, 3.100, 4.600, 6.100, …

       Following is an example of creating a snapping grid from 0.100 with 1.500 interval:
       { USERGRID grid1 0.100 1.500 }
       Following is the output of the above example:
       grid1: 0.100, 1.600, 3.100, 4.600, 6.100, …

       Following is an example of creating a snapping grid from 20.000 with 2.200 interval:
       { USERGRID grid2 20.000 2.200 }
       Following is the output of the above example:
       grid2: 20.000, 22.200, 24.400, 26.600, 28.800, 31.000, …

You can use a predefined USERGRID inside a SNAPTO phrase in either a LAYER or a METAL
statement. This indicates the snapping grid for all wires in the corresponding LAYER or METAL
respectively. See the example below of snapping all wires in the M1 layer to the grid1 routing grid:
       { USERGRID grid1 0.100 1.500 }

       … { LAYER M1 { SNAPTO USERGRID grid1 } … }



SNAPTO Statement

The SNAPTO statement defines the PG wire snapping behavior, when it is used in the design. It
allows snapping of wires to the:
       Predefined TRACKs
       Half-grid in between two TRACKs
       Mask1 or Mask2 grid
       User-specified grid, as indicated in the USERGRID statement
The SNAPTO statement defined in the LAYER statement is applied to all the METALs inside the
LAYER statement, unless the SNAPTO statement is also defined in the METAL statement (where
one in METAL statement will override the behavior defined by the LAYER statement). Following is
the SNAPTO statement in a PSDL file:




Last Updated in September 2025                             614                              Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing



{ SNAPTO ( NONE | GRID | HALFGRID | MASK (1|2) | USERGRID gridName
           | { USERGRID statement } ) [ PATTERNCENTER ]
           | { NONPREFERRED ( LAYER layername ( GRID | HALFGRID ) | USERGRID gridName
)}

Following are the SNAPTO statement snapping options:
       NONE: Prevents snapping of a metal layer to any grid. If you specify the SNAPTO statement
       with the NONE option in a metal layer, it disables snapping in it.
       GRID: Snaps the center of PG wires to the routing tracks, as defined in the design.

       HALFGRID: Snaps the center of the PG wires to the center line between two routing tracks.

       MASK 1|2: Snaps the center of the PG wires to the center line of MASK1 and MASK2.

       USERGRID gridName: Snaps the center of the PG wires to the predefined user grid (gridName)
       that has been defined previously in the USERGRID statement.
       { USERGRID statement }: Creates a user-defined grid first, as specified in the inline
       USERGRID statement, and then uses this grid to snap the center of the PG wires. This newly
       created grid can also be used for snapping for other layers or metals, which will be defined
       later.
       PATTERNCENTER: Snaps the pattern center to either GRID, HALFGRID, or USERGRID based on
       the specified snapping option. When this option is used, the wires in the pattern maintain their
       pattern-specified spacing.
       NONPREFERRED: Snaps the staple wire pattern center of the staple wires to non-preferred
       direction grid, which is allowed only in the LAYER and/or METAL statement. Define at least
       one right-way snapping value with this option, when non-preferred snapping is to be defined.

             NONE is available only when right-way snapping is not needed.



      Flash PG allows two selections for snapping PG wires. You can snap to the closest specified
      selection (either right or left) from the specified GRID, HALFGRID, MASK (1|2), and
      USERGRID options.

See the examples below:
       Following is an example of using the NONPREFERRED option to snap the staple wire pattern
       center of the staple wires to non-preferred direction grid:




Last Updated in September 2025                             615                              Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing




       Following is an example of the SNAPTO statement in the definition of various metal layers in
       a PSDL file:
       { USERGRID grid1 0.100 1.200 }                 # Creating user grid grid1 with offset 0.100 and
       step 1.200
       { REGION
                { LAYER M5 { SNAPTO GRID }
                      { METAL m5_one … }             # m5_one will be snapped to routing TRACKS (same
       as ONGRIDONLY )
                      { METAL m5_two … { SNAPTO NONE } }                   # m5_two will not be snapped to
       any grid
                      { METAL m5_three … { SNAPTO USERGRID grid1 } } }                        # m5_three will be
       snapped to user grid grid1
                { LAYER M6
                      { METAL m6_one … { SNAPTO { USERGRID grid2 0.200 2.400 } } }                          #
       m6_one will be snapped to user grid grid2
                      { METAL m6_two … { SNAPTO USERGRID grid2 } } }                        # m6_two will be
       snapped to user grid grid2

       Following is an example of the SNAPTO statement while allowing snapping pattern center to
       GRID:
       { USERGRID grid1 0.100 1.500 } #create grid1: 0.100, 1.600, 3.100, 4.600, 6.100, …
       { REGION { COREAREA }
            …
            { LAYER METAL3 { SNAPTO USERGRID grid1 PATTERNCENTER }




Last Updated in September 2025                             616                                     Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing



                  { METAL m3_vdd m3_staple { NET VDD } { FOLLOW m1_vdd m0_vdd} { ALIGNMENT
       CENTER CENTER } }
            }
       }
       Following is the output of the above example:

       route_pg

       Components of a PSDL File
       PG Structure Description Language
       PSDL Examples


AREA Statement
Following is the AREA statement in a PSDL file:
{ AREA areaSetId
  [{ INSIDE
       { xL1 yL1 xH1 yH1 }
     | { x1 y1 x2 y2 x3 y3 x4 y4 ... }
     | { POWERDOMAIN ( * | PDName ... ) }
  }]
  [{ EXPAND (
         allSide
       | leftRightSide topBottomSide
       | left bottom right top
       | { LEFT left } { BOTTOM bottom } { RIGHT right } { TOP top }




Last Updated in September 2025                             617                              Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing



     )
  }]
  [{ MERGE distance
     [ (METALDIRECTION ( VERTICAL | HORIZONTAL | ALL )) | ( LAYER layer1 ... )]
  }]
  (    { xL1 yL1 xH1 yH1 }
     | { x1 y1 x2 y2 x3 y3 x4 y4 ... }
     | { CELL cellMasterName ... }
     | { BLOCK ( * | blockName | blockinstanceName ...) }
     | { INSTANCE instanceName ... }
     | { POWERDOMAIN ( * | powerDomainName ... ) }
     | {TCLVAR tclVarName}
  ) ...
| { COREAREA }
  | { PADAREA }
  | { COREIO }
  | { ROWONLY }
}

Here:
         { INSIDE }: Specifies that the AREA defined will be the specified cellMasterName/blockname
         inside the lists of boxes/polygons or power domains.

              Select the cells/blocks that are fully enclosed in the area. Warning will be written in the
              log file for the cells/blocks, which are partially covered in the area.

         MERGE distance specifies the distance between the nearest
         boxes/CELL/BLOCK/INSTANCE/POWERDOMAIN to be merged. Additionally, certain metal
         layers/all vertical direction metals/all horizontal direction metals/all metal layers need to be
         extended for boxes/CELL/BLOCK/INSTANCE/POWERDOMAIN that meet the merge
         distance. The default value of METALDIRECTION is ALL.
See the examples below:
         Following is an example of allowing CELL within certain boxes of areas or power domains in
         the REGION statement:
         { REGION
             { AREA boxes_area {INSIDE { POWERDOMAIN PD1} } {CELL testCell } }
             { LAYER METAL2 { SNAPTO GRID }
                    { METAL m2_vdd m2_staple { NET VDD } { OFFSET 4.435 6} { STEPDISTANCE 8.55
         12 } }




Last Updated in September 2025                             618                              Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing



                  { METAL m2_vss m2_staple { NET VSS } { OFFSET 4.435 12} { STEPDISTANCE
       17.10 12 } }
            }
            { LAYER METAL3 { SNAPTO GRID }
                  { METAL m3_vdd m3_staple { NET VDD } { OFFSET 4.435 6} { STEPDISTANCE 8.55
       12 } }
                  { METAL m3_vss m3_staple { NET VSS } { OFFSET 4.435 12} { STEPDISTANCE
       17.10 12 } }
            }
       }
       Following is the output of the above example:




       Following is an example of measuring OFFSET from each each lower-left most corner of
       REGION boxes with the MERGE distance value as 10.5 and METALDIRECTION as
       VERTICAL:
       { REGION
            { AREA m6_m17_area { MERGE 10.5 METALDIRECTION VERTICAL }
                  {30 60 60 80} {30 30 60 50} }
            { LAYER METAL2 { SNAPTO GRID }
                  { METAL m2_pat m2_stripe { NET VDD VSS }
                       { OFFSET 4.435 * FROM REGION } { STEPDISTANCE 8.55 * } }
            }
            { LAYER METAL3
                  { METAL m3_pat m3_stripe { NET VDD VSS }




Last Updated in September 2025                             619                              Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing



                  { OFFSET * 1.0 FROM REGION } { STEPDISTANCE * 1.5 } }
            }
       }
       Following is the output of the above example:




       Following is an example of measuring OFFSET from each each lower-left most corner of
       REGION boxes with the MERGE distance value as 25 and METALDIRECTION as
       HORIZONTAL:
       { REGION
            { AREA boxes_area { MERGE 25 METALDIRECTION HORIZONTAL }
                  {30 60 60 80} {50 60 80 100} }
            { LAYER METAL2 { SNAPTO GRID }
                  { METAL m2_pat m2_stripe { NET VDD VSS } { OFFSET 4.435 * FROM REGION } {
       STEPDISTANCE 8.55 * } }
            }
            { LAYER METAL3
                  { METAL m3_pat m3_stripe { NET VDD VSS } { OFFSET * 1.0 FROM REGION } {
       STEPDISTANCE * 1.5 } }
            }




Last Updated in September 2025                             620                              Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing



       }
       Following is the output of the above example:




Following are the rectilinear area source options used in the AREA statement:
       { xL1 yL1 xH1 yH1 }: Indicates the coordinates of a rectangle specified by its two corner
       points.
       { x1 y1 x2 y2 x3 y3 x4 y4 … }: Indicates a rectilinear polygon specified by a list of points
       tracing the polygon boundary.
       { CELL cellMasterName … }: Specifies the complete instance area whose cell master matches
       with cellMasterName in the list.
       { BLOCK ( * | blockName | blockinstanceName ...) }: Specifies the complete BLOCK
       MACRO type instance that matches with blockName in the list, or with all BLOCK MACROs, if *
       is specified.
       { INSTANCE instanceName … }: Specifies the complete instance area that matches with the
       instanceName in the list.

       { POWERDOMAIN ( * | powerDomainName … ) }: Specifies the complete power domain region
       that matches with the powerDomainName in the list, or with all the non-default power domains, if
       * is specified.
       { TCLVAR tclVarName }: Specifies that the value of the tclVarName Tcl variable will be used to
       define the rectilinear area for the specified AREA statement. The Tcl variable value must be
       set according to one of the following formats:
              set tclVarName { xL1 yL1 xH1 yH1 }: Indicates setting a simple rectangular area




Last Updated in September 2025                             621                              Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing




              defined by ( xL1 , yL1 ) – ( xH1 , yH1 ).

              set tclVarName { x1 y1 x2 y2 x3 y3 x4 y4 … }: Indicates setting a single rectilinear

              polygon area defined by ( x1 , y1 ), ( x2 , y2 ), ( x3 , y3 ), ( x4 , y4 ), … .

              set tclVarName { ( { xL1 yL1 xH1 yH1 } | { x1 y1 x2 y2 x3 y3 x4 y4 … } ) … }:

              Indicates setting an area that is defined by the union of multiple rectangles and/or
              rectilinear polygons.
The following figure presents the special area definition for the REGION statement:




Following is the AREA Rectangle/Polygon statement in a PSDL file:
{ AREA areaSetId
     [ { EXPAND ( allSide | leftRightSide topBottomSide | left bottom right top
                 | { LEFT left } { BOTTOM bottom } { RIGHT right } { TOP top } ) } ]
     ( { xL1 yL1 xH1 yH1 } | { x1 y1 x2 y2 x3 y3 x4 y4 … } ) … )
}

Following is the AREA CELL/BLOCK/INSTANCE statement in a PSDL file:
{ AREA areaSetId
     [ { EXPAND ( allSide | leftRightSide topBottomSide | left bottom right top
                 | { LEFT left } { BOTTOM bottom } { RIGHT right } { TOP top } ) } ]
     ( { CELL cellMasterName … }
           | { BLOCK ( * | blockName | blockinstanceName ...) }
           | { INSTANCE instanceName … }
}

Following is the AREA POWERDOMAIN statement in a PSDL file:



Last Updated in September 2025                             622                              Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing



{ AREA areaSetId
     [ { EXPAND ( allSide | leftRightSide topBottomSide | left bottom right top
                 | { LEFT left } { BOTTOM bottom } { RIGHT right } { TOP top } ) } ]
     { POWERDOMAIN ( * | powerDomainName … ) }
}



Related Topics
       route_pg

       Components of a PSDL File
       PG Structure Description Language
       PSDL Examples


EXCEPTAREA Statement
Following is the EXCEPTAREA statement in a PSDL file:
{ EXCEPTAREA exceptAreaId
   ( { AREA areaSetId } | { AREA statement }
           | { CELL cellMasterName … }
           | { BLOCK ( * | blockName … ) }
           | { INSTANCE instanceName … }
           | { POWERDOMAIN ( * | powerDomainName … ) } )
}

Following are the exclusion area definition options used in the EXCEPTAREA statement:
       { AREA areaSetId }: Uses the predefined area specified by the areaSetId option in the AREA
       statement.
       { AREA statement }: Defines a new AREA statement and uses it for the EXCEPTAREA
       statement.
       { CELL cellMasterName … }: Excludes all the instance area whose cell master matches with
       cellMasterName in the list.

       { BLOCK ( * | blockName … ) }: Excludes all the BLOCK MACRO type instance that
       matches with blockName in the list, or with all BLOCK MACROs, if * is specified.
       { INSTANCE instanceName … }: Excludes all the instance area that matches with
       instanceName in the list.




Last Updated in September 2025                             623                              Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing



       { POWERDOMAIN ( * | powerDomainName … ) }: Excludes all the power domain region that
       matches with powerDomainName in the list, or with all non-default power domains, if * is
       specified.
Following is the EXCEPTAREA statement with named area definition:
{ EXCEPTAREA exceptAreaId
     { AREA areaSetId
           [ { EXPAND ( allSide | leftRightSide topBottomSide | left bottom right top
                | { LEFT left } { BOTTOM bottom } { RIGHT right } { TOP top } ) } ]
           ( ( { xL1 yL1 xH1 yH1 } | { x1 y1 x2 y2 x3 y3 x4 y4 … } | { CELL
cellMasterName … }
                | { BLOCK ( * | blockName … ) }
                | { INSTANCE instanceName … }
                | { POWERDOMAIN ( * | powerDomainName … ) } ) … )
     }
}



Scope of EXCEPTAREA Statement

You can apply EXCEPTAREA in the REGION, LAYER, or METAL statements. The validity scope
of the EXCEPTAREA depends on the statement where it is applied. The following examples show
the different behaviors of EXCEPTAREA based on where it is defined:
       The following example presents keeping EXCEPTAREA X above all the metal stripes:
       { REGION
       { EXCEPTAREA X }
           { LAYER M1 { METAL A }
              { METAL B } }
           { LAYER M2 { METAL C } }
       }
       Following is the output region of the above example:




Last Updated in September 2025                             624                              Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing




       The following example presents keeping EXCEPTAREA X above the METAL B metal stripe
       and below the METAL A and METAL C metal stripes:
       { REGION
       { LAYER M1 { EXCEPTAREA X }
              { METAL A }
              { METAL B } }
           { LAYER M2 { METAL C } }
       }
       Following is the output region of the above example:




       The following example presents keeping EXCEPTAREA X above the METAL A and METAL
       B metal stripes and below the METAL C metal stripe:
       { REGION




Last Updated in September 2025                             625                              Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing



       { LAYER M1 { METAL A }
                { METAL B { EXCEPTAREA X } }
            }
           { LAYER M2 { METAL C } }
       }
       Following is the output region of the above example:

REGION Statement

Following is the REGION statement in a PSDL file:
{ REGION
     [ ( { AREA areaSetId [ { INSIDE { xL1 yL1 xH1 yH1 } | { x1 y1 x2 y2 x3 y3 x4 y4 … }
| { POWERDOMAIN ( * | PDName … ) } } ]
         [ { MERGE distance [ ( METALDIRECTION ( VERTICAL | HORIZONTAL | ALL )) ] } ] }
| { AREA statement } | { CELL cellMasterName … }
           | { BLOCK ( * | blockName | blockinstanceName … ) } | { INSTANCE
instanceName … }




Last Updated in September 2025                             626                              Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing



                  | { POWERDOMAIN ( * | powerDomainName … ) }
                  | { DESIGNAREA [ PARTIALOUTSIDE ] | COREAREA | PADAREA } ) ]
                  [ { DELETEFLOATING } ]
                  [ { GROUP {metalPatternIda metalPatternIdb } {metalPatternIdc metalPatternIdd
metalPatternIde } {...} } ]
       [ { EXCEPTAREA statement } ]
       [ { UDA udaName } ]
       [ { RING statements } ... ]
       [ { LAYER statements } … ]
       [ { TEMPLATE statements } … ]
            { VIASTACK ( LAYER layerNames | metalNames | { metalNames1 metalNames2 …
metalNamesm } )
                      TO ( LAYER layerNamet | metalNamet | { metalNamet1 metalNamet2 …
metalNametn } )
                      [ { LAYERSNAPTO ( * | layerNamei | { layerNamei1 layerNamei2 …
layerNameip } )
                        { ( GRID | HALFGRID | MASK (1|2) | USERGRID gridName ) … } } … ]
                      [ { VIACELL x1 x2 … xn } ] [ { VIARULE r1 r2 … rn } ] [ { UDA udaName }
]
              }
}

Following are the area definition options used in the REGION statement:
       { AREA areaSetId }: Uses the predefined area specified as areaSetId from the AREA
       statement.
       { INSIDE }: Specifies that the AREA defined will be the specified cellMasterName/blockname
       inside the lists of boxes/polygons or power domains.

             Select the cells/blocks that are fully enclosed in the area. Warning will be written in the
             log file for the cells/blocks, which are partially covered in the area, and the intersection
             center is inside the REGION area.

       { MERGE distance }: Specifies the distance between the nearest
       boxes/CELL/BLOCK/INSTANCE/POWERDOMAIN to be merged. Here, certain metal
       layers/all vertical direction metals/all horizontal direction metals/all metal layers need to be
       extended for boxes/CELL/BLOCK/INSTANCE/POWERDOMAIN that meet the merge
       distance. The default value of METALDIRECTION is ALL.
       { AREA statement }: Defines a new AREA statement and uses it for the REGION area.




Last Updated in September 2025                             627                              Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing



       { CELL cellMasterName … }: Specifies the REGION instance area consisting of all the
       instance area regions, which has its cell master, which matches with cellMasterName.
       { BLOCK ( * | blockName | blockinstanceName … ) }: Specifies the REGION area
       consisting of all the BLOCK MACRO type instances, which matches with blockName or
       blockinstanceName in the list, or all BLOCK MACROs, if * is specified.
       { INSTANCE instanceName … }: Specifies the REGION area consisting of all the instance area
       regions, which matches with instanceName in the list.
       { POWERDOMAIN ( * | powerDomainName … ) }: Specifies the REGION area consisting of all
       the power domain regions, which matches with the powerDomainName in the list, or all non-
       default power domains, if * is specified.
       [ PARTIALOUTSIDE ]: Allows REGION to generate stripes partially outside the DESIGNAREA,
       when their center lines are inside/on the DESIGNAREA. This option is available only for the
       stripes, and not staples. By default, a PG pin will be generated at the edge of the design
       boundary when this option is specified.
       [ DELETEFLOATING ]: Deletes all the generated floating PG wires.

       [ GROUP ]: Groups several metalPatternId values in a region to be generated and removed.
       All the metalPatternId values specified in GROUP need to be defined in the same REGION.
       The GROUP option is useful when a part of the pattern wire/via cannot be generated due to
       region limitation or DRC. In this case, the whole pattern in the set will be removed along with
       any metalPatternId that is in the same group. However, followpin will not be removed
       regardless of the group. Additionally, metalPatternId in a group needs to be from different
       layers.
       { VIASTACK }: Generates stack via in the regions where via-stacking is required.


             By default, this option creates stack via between two generated orthogonal PG wires,
             or between generated PG wires with its orthogonal block/cell pins.

       The width and length of the intermediate layers will be determined by the intersection area of
       the top and bottom layers, which might be elongated depending on the via-enclosure of the
       adjacent layer in the design rules. For example, if you define each LAYER as snap-to-grid, the
       enclosure of vias need to be extended to capture the adjacent vias to be on the GRID. When
       no snapping specified, via-stack will not be snapped to any grid. When a part of the via stack
       fails to be created due to several reasons (such as existing shapes and DRC violations), all
       the intermediate wires and vias for this intersection are removed.




Last Updated in September 2025                             628                              Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing



       [ { VIACELL x1 x2 … xn } ] [ { VIARULE r1 r2 … rn } ]: Specify VIACELLs and/or
       VIARULEs to be used with the VIASTACK option. When these options are specified, the
       software selects the first DRC clean from the predefined via cell. If none of the vias specified
       in the list can provide a DRC clean solution, then a regular via will be generated based on the
       surrounding metal that can bring maximum cut area.

             These options create via stack between two generated orthogonal PG wires or
             between the generated PG wires with its orthogonal blocks/cells pins, by default. Use
             the route_pg -skip_block_pin parameter to omit via-stack generation between the
             generated PG wires and orthogonal blocks’ pin.

While using the VIASTACK option for the source and target layers:
       By default, via stack is generated between two generated orthogonal PG wires or between
       generated PG wires and blocks’ or cells’ pin, where its intersection center is inside the
       REGION area.
       metalNames1, metalNames2 , … metalNamesm must be on the same source layer.

       metalNamet1 , metalNamet2 , … metalNametn must be on the same target layer.

       Apply this option only for the previously defined metals blocks’/cells’ pin.
While using the VIASTACK option for an intermediate layer:
       layerNamei1 , layerNamei2 , … layerNameip must be intermediate layers between the source

       and target layers.
       You can specify more than one snapping modes, from which the closest one will be adopted.
       The snapping modes for wires on an intermediate layer:
              Are decided by the first LAYERSNAPTO keyword defined in the VIASTACK statement.

              Are decided by the LAYER statement for this layer in this region when no
              LAYERSNAPTO statement is defined in the VIASTACK statement.
              Does not perform snapping when nothing is defined in LAYERSNAPTO in the
              VIASTACK statement or SNAPTO in the LAYER statement.
The behavior of the VIASTACK option is presented in the figure below:




Last Updated in September 2025                             629                              Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing




See the following examples of the REGION statement:
       Following is an example of specifying preferred vias, where via-stack is needed from the
       m5_vss to m8_vss metal Ids. The via preferences in VIASTACK option in this example are for
       transition cut layers. Any via preferences not in the transition cut layer will be ignored:
       { PATTERN m5_pat {TYPE STRIPE} {DIRECTION VERTICAL} {WIDTH 0.018} }
       { PATTERN m8_pat {TYPE STRIPE} {DIRECTION HORIZONTAL} {WIDTH 0.1} }
       { REGION { COREAREA}
            { LAYER METAL8
                  { METAL m8_vdd m8_pat { NET VDD } { OFFSET * 0.2} { STEPDISTANCE * 0.8 } }
                  { METAL m8_vss m8_pat { NET VSS } { OFFSET * 0.6} { STEPDISTANCE * 0.8 } }
            }
            { LAYER METAL5
                  { METAL m5_vdd m5_pat { NET VDD } { OFFSET 0.245 *} { STEPDISTANCE 1.152 *
       } {FOLLOW * m8_vdd} {ALIGNMENT * CENTER} }
                  { METAL m5_vss m5_pat { NET VSS } { OFFSET 0.245 *} { STEPDISTANCE 1.152 *
       } {FOLLOW * m8_vss} {ALIGNMENT * CENTER} }
            }
            { VIASTACK m5_vss TO m8_vss { VIACELL V7BAR_1x1 V5_1 V5_2 } }

       }

       while VSS net has V7BAR_1x1, V5_1, and V5_2 as preferred vias:

RING Statement

Following is the RING statement in the REGION statement:
{ RING ringName
    { INNER | OUTER } [ { UDA udaName } ]
    { NET [ USEGROUP ] net1 net2 … netn }
     { EXTENDWIRE ( AUTO | NONE ) }
     { LAYER ( { LEFT layerLeft } { BOTTOM layerBottom } { RIGHT layerRight } { TOP
layerTop } | layerAll ) }
    { WIDTH ( allSideWidth | leftRightWidth topBottomWidth | left bottom right top
                 | { LEFT left } { BOTTOM bottom } { RIGHT right } { TOP top } ) }
    { OFFSET ( allSideOffset | leftRightOffset topBottomOffset | left bottom right top
                 | { LEFT left } { BOTTOM bottom } { RIGHT right } { TOP top } ) }
    { SPACING ( allSideSpacing | leftRightSpacing topBottomSpacing | left bottom right
top
                 | { LEFT left } { BOTTOM bottom } { RIGHT right } { TOP top } ) }
}


      EXTENDWIRE NONE does not extend any PG wires to the PG rings, unless specified in the
      METAL statement.

See the examples below:
       Following is an example of the INNER RING usage:
       { RING { INNER } { LAYER M2 M2 M1 M1 }
       { NET VDD VSS } { WIDTH w } { OFFSET o } { SPACING s } }
       Following is the output of the above example:

                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing

       { RING { OUTER }
       { LAYER M2 M2 M1 M1 }
            { NET VDD VSS } { WIDTH w }
            { OFFSET o } { SPACING s } }

LAYER Statement

       METAL Statement
              FOLLOW and ALIGNMENT Statements
       VIA Statement
Following is the LAYER statement in the REGION statement:
{ LAYER layerName
     [ { ONGRIDONLY } | { SNAPTO statement } ]
     [ { UDA udaName } ]
     { METAL statements } …
     [ { EXCEPTAREA statement } ]




Last Updated in September 2025                             644                              Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--Power Planning and Routing



}




METAL Statement
Metal specifies the location within the region where the patterns will be used to develop the
specified design. It contains the offset and distance between the various patterns within the defined
region. Following is the METAL statement in the LAYER statement:
{ METAL metalPatternId patternName
  { NET net1 net2 … netn } [ { UDA udaName } ]
    [ { EXTENDTO ( ringName1 ringName2 ...                | { DESIGNAREA value } ) } ]
    [ { SIZE ( * | xnum ) ( * | ynum ) } ]               [ { SNAPTO statement } ] ]
    [ { OFFSET x0 y0 [ FROM ( REGION | ZERO | CORE | DESIGN | CELL | REGIONBBOX ) ] [ TO (
CENTER | LOWEDGE) ] } ]
  [ { STEPDISTANCE xdist ydist [ BEFORESNAPPING ] [ { LOWEDGETOLOWEDGE |
       CENTERTOCENTER | EDGETOEDGE } ] } ]
    [ { FOLLOW syntax } ]
    [ { ALIGNMENT syntax } ]
    [ { EXCEPTAREA statement } ]
}

Following are the attributes used in the METAL statement:
       { NET net1 net2 … netn }: Specifies the net attached to the corresponding width in the

       PATTERN statement. The net set in the METAL statement is a secondary set, where the
       width set in the corresponding PATTERN statement is the primary set. This means that the
       number of nets in the set follows the number of width sets in the pattern.
       { UDA statement }: Specifies the user-defined attributes for all the PG wire objects created by
       the associated METAL statement. The UDA statement overrides both the LAYER UDA and
       REGION UDA attributes.
       { EXTENDTO ( ringName1 ringName2 ...                | { DESIGNAREA value } ) }: Specifies the
       specific METAL stripe to be extended to the specified ringName or the design boundary, when
       the distance between the REGION boundary and design boundary is lesser than or equal to
       the threshold value. This option works only when the the specified ringName has EXTENDWIRE
       NONE property.


             The extension of stripes to the design boundary is available only for the region with a
             single instance and STRIPE pattern.




Last Updated in September 2025                             645                              Product Version 23.16
                                      Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                   Design Planning Capabilities--METAL Statement



       { SIZE ( * | xnum ) ( * | ynum ) }: Specifies the number of times the PG wires set are

       replicated in the specified REGION. By default (with default value = *), the PG wires’ set is
       replicated until it reaches the end of the REGION.
       { SNAPTO statement }: Specifies the snapping behavior of all the PG wires generated by the
       METAL statement. This overrides the LAYER snapping attribute.
       { OFFSET x0 y0 [ FROM ( REGION | CORE | DESIGN | ZERO | CELL |REGIONBBOX) ] [ TO (

       CENTER | LOWEDGE) ]}: Specifies the absolute starting offset for the PG wires’ set. The offset
       value is calculated based on one or more of the following coordinates:
              REGION’s lower-left coordinate of union of multiple areas (default value)
              Lower-left coordinate of CORE bounding box
              Lower-left coordinate of DESIGN bounding box
              Absolute (0,0) coordinate
              Lower-left coordinate of each CELL/instance/block
              REGIONBBOX specifies the union of bounding box
              OFFSET measurement from the lower-edge of the first metal in the pattern


                           The default value of OFFSET is REGIONBBOX when no value is specified
                           for OFFSET. This REGIONBBOX merges all the CELL, BOX, INSTANCE,
                           or POWERDOMAIN area regions, and uses the lowest-left corner of the
                           merged boxes as the initial OFFSET.
                           The default value of OFFSET is TO LOWEDGE in all other cases. When
                           you specify TO CENTER, the OFFSET measurement is done from the
                           lowest-left edge of the region to the center of the pattern bounding box.
                           You can measure the OFFSET from each lowest-left corner of REGION
                           boxes by specifying the FROM REGION and FROM CELL keywords.


       { EXCEPTAREA statement }: Specifies the exclusion area in which all the PG wires generated
       by the METAL statement need to be avoided. This exclusion region is added on the top of the
       EXCEPTAREA statement in both the LAYER and REGION statements.
       { FOLLOW statement }: Specifies the METAL to be used as a reference for both the starting
       offset and set-to-set distance.




Last Updated in September 2025                          646                             Product Version 23.16
                                     Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                  Design Planning Capabilities--METAL Statement



         { ALIGNMENT statement }: Specifies the alignment behavior between the PG wires’ set from
         the current METAL and the set in the reference METAL.
         { STEPDISTANCE xdist ydist [ BEFORESNAPPING ] [ ( LOWEDGETOLOWEDGE | CENTERTOCENTER

         | EDGETOEDGE ) ] }: Specifies the set-to-set absolute distance between the set of two PG
         wires. The distance is calculated based on their low-edge, default center point, or edge-to-
         edge spacing measurement. By default, the distance is calculated based on the first wire from
         the previous set after when it is snapped to the grid. If BEFORESNAPPING is specified, then
         the distance is calculated based on the first wire from the previous set before it was snapped
         to the grid.

      You need to specify both x and y coordinates with the STEPDISTANCE and OFFSET attributes.


When the PATTERN is specified as STRIPE, you can use (*) to specify the x coordinate for the
horizontal pattern in the STEPDISTANCE and OFFSET attributes. Alternatively, you can specify the y
coordinate for the vertical pattern. See the example below:
{ PATTERN pattern_Z { TYPE STAPLE } { DIRECTION VERTICAL } { WIDTH 0.36 0.36 } {
SPACING 0.4 } { LENGTH 1.69 2.21 } }
{ PATTERN pattern_A { TYPE STRIPE } { DIRECTION VERTICAL } { WIDTH 0.36 } }
{ REGION
    { AREA { xL1 yL1 xH1 yH1 } }
     { LAYER M3
         { METAL example1 pattern_Z
             { NET vdd }
             { OFFSET x0 y0 }
                 { STEPDISTANCE x0dist y0dist }
     }
            { METAL example2 pattern_A
                { NET vdd }
                { OFFSET x1 * }
                 { STEPDISTANCE x1dist * }
            }
     }
}

Following is the output of the above example:

       2.00 } }

VIA Statement
A VIA statement defines the preference order of the via(s) to be used for the connection between
two METAL regions on the adjacent layers. The via type can be specified as VIARULE, VIACELL,
or CUTCLASS, where VIARULE has the highest precedence, and CUTCLASS has the least.
The software chooses the first DRC clean option from the predefined rule via, predefined via cell,
and generated via, first based on the specified via cut-class. If none of the vias are specified in the
list and a DRC clean solution is provided, then a regular via is generated based on the surrounding
metal that can bring maximum cut area.
You can use the VIACELL argument to insert a VIACELL between two different abutted wires
originating from different regions. This functionality is similar to the behavior of the route_pg -
use_namespace parameter.

Following is the use model of the VIA statement:
{ VIA ( ( { { ( VIARULE | VIACELL [ IGNOREDRC ] | CUTCLASS | VIAPATTERN) x1 x2 ... }
... | SKIP }
    [{ SPLIT {THRESHOLD value OFFSET value STEPDISTANCE via_center_to_center_distance [
LENGTH length ]}}]
      | { SPLIT {THRESHOLD value OFFSET value STEPDISTANCE




Last Updated in September 2025                        662                               Product Version 23.16
                                      Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                     Design Planning Capabilities--VIA Statement



via_center_to_center_distance [ LENGTH length ]}} )
       TO ( ALL | BELOW | ABOVE | { metal1 metal2 ... }                      | {PINWIDTH min max [ABOVE |
BELOW]})
           ) ... }

Here:
        IGNOREDRC: Allows you to add your preferred vias without running a DRC check. You can
        run a DRC check later to ensure a DRC clean design.
        SKIP: Skips vias defined in PSDL input file from from generation.
        The OFFSET, STEPDISTANCE, and LENGTH options are used only for two adjacent
        parallel wires.
        OFFSET: Is measured from the lowest-left edge of the region to the center of the pattern
        bounding box.
        PINWIDTH: Enables via preference for the specified pin width. The VIA statement defined in
        the LAYER/REGION statement gives preference to the via(s) to be used for connecting
        between generated METALs on adjacent layers to the pin with certain width. The via type can
        be specified as VIARULE, VIACELL, or CUTCLASS type, where the first one in the list has
        the highest precedence. The software selects the first DRC clean from the predefined rule via,
        the predefined via cell, or the generated via, based on the specified via cut-class first. If none
        of the vias are specified in the list as DRC clean, a regular via is generated based on the
        surrounding metal that can bring maximum cut area. When you define both via preference for
        PINWIDTH and general via preference, the priority will be given to PINWIDTH by general via
        preference.
        LENGTH: Specifies via metal enclosure.
        THRESHOLD/OFFSET/STEPDISTANCE/LENGTH: Split vias between two adjacent parallel
        wires. Here:
              THRESHOLD specifies minimum length of overlapping adjacent parallel wires, which
              will trigger the split long via mechanism.
              OFFSET is measured from via-center to the edge of overlapping adjacent parallel wires.
              LENGTH is the entire length of generated via-metal-enclosure. Do not use this attribute
              when VIACELL is specified.
              STEPDISTANCE is measured from via-center to via-center

                    Via preference is optional for the split-via function.




Last Updated in September 2025                          663                                  Product Version 23.16
                                  Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--VIA Statement



See the examples below:
       Following is an example of specifying VIACELL for abutted wires from different region, which
       is the equivalent functionality of using the -use_namespace parameter of the route_pg
       command:
       set A1 { { 936 927 502 927 502 499 936 499 } }
       set A2 { { 502 499 0 499 0 0 502 0 } }
       { REGION { AREA A1 { TCLVAR A1 } }
            { LAYER M12
                  { METAL m12vdd H1T {NET VDD} … { UDA m12vdd } }
            }
            { LAYER M13
                  { VIA { VIACELL VIA12pg } TO {*::m12vdd} }
                  { METAL m13vdd V4T {NET VDD} … {UDA m13vdd} }
            }
       }
       { REGION { AREA A2 { TCLVAR A2 } }
            { LAYER M12
                  { METAL m12vdd H1T {NET VDD} … { UDA VDD_m12 } }
            }
            { LAYER M13
                  { VIA { VIACELL VIA12pg } TO {*::m12vdd} }
                  { METAL m13vdd V4T {NET VDD} … {UDA VDD_m13} }
            }
       }
       Following are the outputs of the above example:

                                 Design Planning Capabilities--VIA Statement




       Following is an example of using the SKIP option for skipping vias defined in the PSDL input
       file from generation:
       { REGION { COREAREA }
            { LAYER M1 { METAL m1_vdd m1_followpin { NET {TCLVAR vdd_net} } }
                  { METAL m1_vss m1_followpin { NET {TCLVAR gnd_net} } } }
            { LAYER M2 { METAL m2_vdd m2_vdd_staple { NET VDD } { OFFSET 4.435 * } {
       STEPDISTANCE (m2_p) * } { FOLLOW * m1_vdd } { ALIGNMENT * CENTER } }
                  { METAL m2_vss m2_vss_staple { NET VSS } { OFFSET 4.435 * } { STEPDISTANCE
       17.10 * } { FOLLOW * m1_vss } { ALIGNMENT * CENTER } } }
            { LAYER M3 { VIA SKIP TO { m2_vdd } }
                  { METAL m3_vdd m3_staple { NET VDD } { FOLLOW m2_vdd m1_vdd} { ALIGNMENT
       CENTER CENTER } { UDA testm3_vdd } }
                  { METAL m3_vss m3_staple { NET VSS } { FOLLOW m2_vss m1_vss} { ALIGNMENT
       CENTER CENTER } } }
            { LAYER M4
                  { METAL m4_vdd m4_staple { NET VDD } { FOLLOW m2_vdd m1_vdd} { ALIGNMENT
       CENTER CENTER } { VIA SKIP TO ALL } }
            { METAL m4_vss m4_staple { NET VSS } { FOLLOW m2_vss m1_vss} { ALIGNMENT
       CENTER CENTER } } }}
       # VIA23 connection to m2_vdd will not be added ; m4_vdd connection to both
       adjacent top and bottom layers are not generated

       Following are few examples of using the PINWIDTH option:




Last Updated in September 2025                      665                             Product Version 23.16
                                    Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                   Design Planning Capabilities--VIA Statement



              { REGION
                    { LAYER M3
                         { VIA {via23_customA} TO {PINWIDTH 0.0 0.49999} } # 0 ≤ pinwidth ≥
              0.4999
                         { VIA {via23_customB} TO {PINWIDTH 0.5 1.0} } # 0.5 ≤ pinwith ≥ to
              1.0
                    }
              }

              { REGION
                    { LAYER M3
                         { VIA {via23_customB via23_customA} TO {PINWIDTH 0.0 1.0} } # 0 ≤
              pinwidth ≥ 1.0
                    }
              }

              { REGION
                    { LAYER M3
                         { VIA { CUTCLASS SINGLE} TO {PINWIDTH 0.0 1.0 BELOW} } # 0 ≤ pinwidth
              ≥ 1.0 for VIA23 single
                         { VIA { CUTCLASS DOUBLE} TO {PINWIDTH 1.001 2.0 ABOVE} } # 1.001 ≤
              pinwidth ≥ 2.0 for VIA34 double
                    }
              }

              # 0 ≤ pinwidth ≥ 1.0 for via23_custom because of BELOW keyword
              { REGION
                    { LAYER M3
                         { VIA {via23_customB via34_customA} TO {PINWIDTH 0.0 1.0 BELOW} }
                    }
              }

              # first priority will be to generate via34_customA for 0 ≤ pinwidth ≥ 0.5
              followed by via34_customB with viaGen generate as the last resort to achive
              DRC clean via34 connection
              { REGION




Last Updated in September 2025                        666                        Product Version 23.16
                                    Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                   Design Planning Capabilities--VIA Statement



                     { LAYER M3
                         { VIA {via34_customA} TO {PINWIDTH 0.0 0.5} } # 0 ≤ pinwidth ≥ 0.5
                         { VIA { VIACELL VIA34_customB } TO ABOVE }
                     }
                }

       Following is an example of splitting vias between two adjacent parallel wires:
       { REGION { AREA designbox }
            { LAYER M1 { SNAPTO GRID }
                    { METAL m1vdd m1_stripe { NET VDD} { OFFSET * y1} { STEPDISTANCE * xy1} }
                    { METAL m1vss m1_stripe { NET VSS} { OFFSET * y2} { STEPDISTANCE * xy2} }
            }
            { LAYER M2
                    { VIA { VIARULE VIA12 } { SPLIT { 4.0 0.5 STEPDISTANCE 1.0 LENGTH 0.2 } }
       TO { m1vdd m1vss } }
                    { METAL m2vdd m2_stripe { NET VDD } { FOLLOW * m1vdd } { ALIGNMENT *
       CENTER } }
                    { METAL m2vss m2_stripe { NET VSS } { FOLLOW * m1vss } { ALIGNMENT *
       CENTER } }
       }
       Following are the outputs of the above example:




Last Updated in September 2025                        667                               Product Version 23.16
                                  Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--VIA Statement




       Following is an example of using the VIA statement with adding a via preference without
       checking for DRCs:
       { REGION { AREA designbox } { EXCEPTAREA { BLOCK * } }
            { LAYER M1 { SNAPTO GRID }
                  { METAL m1_1 m1_staple1 { NET VDD} { OFFSET x1 y1} { STEPDISTANCE xs1 xy1}
       }
                  { METAL m1_2 m1_staple2 { NET VDD} { OFFSET x2 y2} { STEPDISTANCE xs2 xy2}
       }
                  { METAL m1_3 m1_staple3 { NET VSS } { OFFSET x3 y3} { STEPDISTANCE xs3
       xy3} }
            }
            { LAYER M2 { SNAPTO GRID } { VIA { VIACELL IGNOREDRC VIA12_HV_2 VIA12_HV_1 }
       TO { m1_1 m1_2 } }
                                            { VIACELL VIA12_HV_3 } TO { m1_1 m1_2 } }
                                            { VIARULE VIA12_RULE_big } TO { m1_3 } }
                  { METAL m2_vdd m2_staple { NET VDD } { FOLLOW m1_1 m0_vdd} { ALIGNMENT
       CENTER CENTER } }
                  { METAL m2_vdd2 m2_staple { NET VDD } { FOLLOW m1_2 m0_vdd} { ALIGNMENT
       CENTER CENTER } }
                  { METAL m2_vss m2_staple { NET VSS } { FOLLOW m1_3 m0_vss} { ALIGNMENT
       CENTER CENTER } }
            }
       }




Last Updated in September 2025                      668                            Product Version 23.16
                                     Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                    Design Planning Capabilities--VIA Statement



       In this example, the Flash PG engine will first attempt to use VIA12_HV_2 for connecting
       m1_1/m1_2 with the M2 layer. If this VIACELL will not work for the intersection area, the
       second attempt will be to use VIA12_HV_1 for connecting m1_1/m1_2 with the M2 layer. In
       case these VIACELLs will not work for the intersection area, a third attempt will be to use
       VIA12_HV_3 for connecting m1_1/m1_2 with the M2 layer.
       Following is an example of using the VIA statement within multiple the LAYER statements:
       { REGION { AREA designbox }             { EXCEPTAREA { BLOCK * } }
            { LAYER M1 { SNAPTO GRID }
                   { METAL m1_1 m1_staple1 { NET VDD} { OFFSET x1 y1} { STEPDISTANCE xs1 xy1}
       }
                   { METAL m1_2 m1_staple2 { NET VDD} { OFFSET x2 y2} { STEPDISTANCE xs2 xy2}
       }
                   { METAL m1_3 m1_staple3 { NET VSS } { OFFSET x3 y3} { STEPDISTANCE xs3
       xy3} }
            }
            { LAYER M2 { SNAPTO GRID }
                                 { VIA { VIACELL VIA12_HV_2 VIA12_HV_1 } TO { m1_1 m1_2 } } {
       VIARULE VIA12_RULE_big } TO { m1_3 } }
                { METAL m2_vdd m2_staple { NET VDD } { FOLLOW m1_1 m0_vdd} { ALIGNMENT CENTER
       CENTER } }
                { METAL m2_vdd2 m2_staple { NET VDD } { FOLLOW m1_2 m0_vdd} { ALIGNMENT
       CENTER CENTER } }
                { METAL m2_vss m2_staple { NET VSS }           { FOLLOW m1_3 m0_vss} { ALIGNMENT
       CENTER CENTER } }
            }
       Following is the output of the above example:

                                  Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--VIA Statement




       Following is an example of using the VIA statement within a single LAYER statement:
       { REGION { CORERECT }
            { LAYER METAL1 …}
            { LAYER METAL2 … }
            { LAYER METAL3 … }
            { LAYER METAL4 { SNAPTO GRID }
                  { VIA { VIACELL VIA34_custom } TO { m3_vss } }
                  { METAL m4_vdd m4_staple { NET VDD } { FOLLOW m2_vdd m3_vdd} { ALIGNMENT
       CENTER CENTER } }
                  { METAL m4_vss m4_staple { NET VSS } { FOLLOW m2_vss m3_vss} { ALIGNMENT
       CENTER CENTER } }
            }
        }

       In the following example, Flash PG will search VIA23_* VIACELL in the tech LEF, which will be
       used as via-preference to connect to m2_vss.
       { VIA { VIACELL VIA23_* } TO { m2_vss } }

       In the following example, Flash PG will use VIA34_LONG_H_BW60_UW40 as preferred the via to
       connect to the PG_M3_6T_* metal Id:
       { VIA { VIACELL VIA34_LONG_H_BW60_UW40 } TO { PG_M3_6T_* } }

UDA Statement

Use the UDA (User Defined Attribute) statement to attach a string identifier/name for the PG wires
and vias. This attribute is useful for performing group query at Tcl level, once the complete PG
mesh is generated. Following is the definition of UDA statement:
{ UDA udaName }

You can add one UDA statement to either REGION, RING, LAYER, or METAL statements. The
scope of this attribute assignment depends on the statement, where the UDA statement is added.
PG vias always take the UDA value of the bottom PG wires, where they are connected to. udaName
must follow the C-style variable naming rule (it means, it cannot start with a number).
You can use the UDA statement in a PSDL file in the following ways:
        { REGION { UDA uda1 }
              { LAYER M1 { UDA uda2 }
                   { METAL met1 pat… { UDA uda3 } }
                   { METAL met2 pat… } }
              { LAYER M2 { METAL met3 pat… } } }

Here:
        All wires generated for met1 will have uda3.
        All wires generated for met2 will have uda2 (based on the LAYER’s UDA).
        All wires generated for met3 will have uda1 (based on the REGION’s UDA).




Last Updated in September 2025                       672                            Product Version 23.16
                                  Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--VIA Statement




Related Topics
       route_pg

       Components of a PSDL File
       PG Structure Description Language
       PSDL Examples


TEMPLATE Statement

The TEMPLATE statement enables reuse of the same PG structure across different net names or
regions. To apply this, you first need to define the TEMPLATE statement as a user-defined PG
structure. It can then be referenced in the REGION statement, allowing the same PG structure to be
used with different net names.
Following is the TEMPLATE statement definition in the REGION statement:
{ TEMPLATE A { VARIABLE net1 net2 uda1 uda2 }
    { LAYER M0 { METAL met_id1 pat0 { NET ( $net1 ) } { UDA ( $uda1 ) } }
        { METAL met_id2 pat0 { NET ( $net2 ) } { UDA ( $uda2 ) } } } ….
}

You can use the defined template repeatedly in a single PSDL input file with different net names, as
shown below:
{ REGION { POWERDOMAIN A }
    {TEMPLATE A { VARIABLE {net1 VDDA} {net2 VSS} { uda1 met_id1_A } { uda2 met_id2_A }
} }
}
{ REGION { POWERDOMAIN B }
    {TEMPLATE A { VARIABLE {net1 VDDB} {net2 VSS} { uda1 met_id1_B } { uda2 met_id2_B }
} }
}



Example
Following is an example of using the TEMPLATE statement for repeating the same PG structure
with different net names:
{ TEMPLATE A { VARIABLE net1 net2 uda1 uda2 uda3 uda4 }
    { LAYER M0
        { METAL MET_M0_VDD PAT_M0 { NET ( $net1 ) } { UDA ( $uda1 ) } }
        { METAL MET_M0_VSS PAT_M0 { NET ( $net2 ) } { UDA ( $uda2 ) } }




Last Updated in September 2025                      673                            Product Version 23.16
                                  Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 Design Planning Capabilities--VIA Statement



      }
      { LAYER M1 { ONGRIDONLY }
         { VIA { VIACELL VIA01_1cut18_BW20_UW28 } TO { MET_M0_VDD MET_M0_VSS } }
         { METAL MET_M1_VDD PAT_M1 { NET ( $net1 ) } { OFFSET 0.346 * } { STEPDISTANCE
0.384 * } { FOLLOW * MET_M0_VDD } { ALIGNMENT * {OFFSET -0.002} } { UDA ( $uda3 ) } }
         { METAL MET_M1_VSS PAT_M1 { NET ( $net2 ) } { OFFSET 0.346 * } { STEPDISTANCE
0.384 * } { FOLLOW * MET_M0_VSS } { ALIGNMENT * {OFFSET 0.002} } { UDA ( $uda4 ) } }
    }
}
######## USING TEMPLATE in many different REGION with different PG net name ##########
{ REGION { POWERDOMAIN A }
    {TEMPLATE A { VARIABLE {net1 VDDA} {net2 VSS} { uda1 met0_A_vdd } { uda2 met0_A_vss
} { uda3 met1_A_vdd } { uda4 met1_A_vss } } }
}
{ REGION { POWERDOMAIN B }
    {TEMPLATE A { VARIABLE {net1 VDDB} {net2 VSS} { uda1 met0_B_vdd } { uda2 met0_B_vss
} { uda3 met1_B_vdd } { uda4 met1_B_vss } } }
}
