Related Information
     Using the NanoRoute Router


Routing Wide Wires
The NanoRoute router automatically tapers wide wires when connecting to pins, including input/output pins of standard cells, macro cells, and block
output pins. The tapered portion of a wire uses the minimum-width wire (the default width). If you do not want tapering at the output pins, specify the
following:
set_db route_detail_no_taper_on_output_pin true

Note: By default, the NanoRoute router prohibits tapering on top level pins.


Using Non-Default Rules
By default, the NanoRoute router treats non-default rule spacing as a soft option; that is, when routing resources are available, it honors the non-
default rule. If the area is too congested, and resources are not available, the router might not honor the rule. If you enable signal-integrity driven
routing, the router attempts to minimize overall coupling capacitance in the design. If you enable timing-driven routing, the router also favors critical
nets when choosing spacing. You can use up to 254 nondefault rules.
Note: You define nondefault routing rules in the NONDEFAULTRULE statement of the LEF file.
Nondefault rules do not necessarily decrease the routing speed. Routing speed does decrease, however, due to the following factors:
     The ratio of non-default rule wires to default rule wires increases.
     The amount of space between wires increases.
     The number of additional nondefault vias increases, due to the nondefault rules.
In congested areas, the router might violate nondefault spacing rules in order to avoid design-rule violations and complete the routing. Its flexibility
with regard to nondefault spacing decreases the overall wirelength and benefits timing and signal integrity because it allows some shorter nets to be
more easily tolerated near adjacent nets without causing violations.
Example: The following illustration shows non-default spacing ("soft spacing") routing.




You can force the router to honor the nondefault rules by specifying the following option:
set_db route_strict_honor_route_rule true

Note: Setting the rule to false will attempt to honor the NDR rule and allow short hot spots without detouring. If you set the rule to true (hard rule) we
recommend you work more on minimizing the congested areas to preserve the NDR topology.


Related Information
     Using the NanoRoute Router




Last Updated in September 2025                                              990                                                    Product Version 23.16
                                                  Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                      Design Implementation Capabilities--Using the NanoRoute Router




Repairing Process Antenna Violations
The NanoRoute router can repair process antenna violations concurrently with DRC violations during the search-and-repair step. During the postroute
optimization step, when there are no more DRC violations, the router repairs additional process antenna violations. This two-step methodology allows
the router to use more aggressive methods to repair the process antenna violations early on and saves CPU time. During postroute optimization, the
router repairs antenna violations by changing layers (also called antenna stapling or layer hopping). It also repairs process antenna violations by
inserting diode cells as close as possible to input gates to discharge current, and deleting and rerouting nets with violations.
Note: After routing, run the connect_global_net command to ensure connectivity to power and ground pins in antenna cells added during process
antenna repair.
The router supports hierarchical process antenna calculations and repair.


Repairing Violations on Multiple-Pin Nets

On multiple-pin nets, the router does the following:
     On a two-pin net that has one input pin with antenna information and one output pin without antenna information, the router tries to repair the
     antenna violation based on input antenna information only.
     On a two-pin net that has one input pin without antenna information and one output pin with antenna information, there is usually no antenna
     violation on the output pin, so the router skips antenna repair.
     On a two pin-net where the router does not have any antenna information on either pin, the router skips antenna repair.
     On a three-pin net that has two input pins – one with antenna information and one without antenna information – and one output pin without
     antenna information, the router skips antenna repair.


Changing Layers
The router automatically shortens wires whose area exceeds the gate/wire area ratio set in the LEF file. This process might not guarantee that it can
resolve all antenna violations – if the routing area is congested, process antenna violations can still occur, just as shorts and spacing violations can
occur.


Using Diodes

The router inserts antenna diode cells or uses preplaced diode cells to repair violations. It can swap filler cells with antenna diode cells and fill the gap
automatically if an antenna diode cell is not the same size as the filler cell it replaced. A later routing pass does not remove previously placed
diodes. The antenna diode cells must have the same LEF SITE definition as the standard cells. Specify the diode cell name using the Diode Cell
Name option on the NanoRoute form or the route_antenna_cell_name option on the text command line.


Deleting and Rerouting Nets with Violations
If the design has more than 100 DRC violations, and you are using LEF 5.4 or later, the router deletes and reroutes nets with process antenna
violations.


Repairing Violations on Cut Layers
The NanoRoute router detects antenna violations on cut layers and repairs them by inserting diodes. To repair these violations, you must specify a
value in your LEF file for the ANTENNADIFFAREARATIO (or ANTENNACUMDIFFAREARATIO) for the cut layers. For each cut layer, the value for
ANTENNADIFFAREARATIO (or ANTENNACUMDIFFAREARATIO) must be larger than the value for ANTENNAAREARATIO (or ANTENNACUMAREARATIO).


     If you do not use diodes to repair process antenna violations, the router cannot repair the violations on cut layers.




Last Updated in September 2025                                              991                                                    Product Version 23.16
                                                 Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                     Design Implementation Capabilities--Using the NanoRoute Router




           To highlight the diodes that the router inserts, choose Edit - Select by Name.
           To highlight the diodes, type *_antenna_* .
           To specify the diode cells, use set_db route_antenna_cell_name.
           To force the router to do more layer changing and skip diode insertion, specify set_db route_antenna_diode_insertion false
           After the router repairs as many violations as possible by layer changing, reset this option to true and repeat process antenna repair.



Process Antenna Options
Use the following options to repair violations caused by process antennas:
     route Category Attributes:

           route_detail_fix_antenna

           route_antenna_cell_name

           route_ignore_antenna_top_cell_pin

           route_antenna_diode_insertion

           route_diode_insertion_for_clock_nets

     set_route_attributes -nets netName -skip_antenna_fix

Examples
     The following commands show the basic strategy for repairing process antenna violations:
     set_db route_detail_fix_antenna true
     set_db route_antenna_cell_name "ANTENNA"
     set_db route_antenna_diode_insertion true
     route_design
     connect_global_net


     The NanoRoute router runs global and detailed routing. After repairing DRC violations, it repairs as many process antenna violations as it can
     by layer hopping during postroute optimization. If any process antenna violations remain, the router repairs them by inserting antenna diode cells
     named ANTENNA.
     The following commands repair process antenna violations by inserting diodes and filler cells. The filler cells are specified by the set_db
     add_fillers_cells command. They fill any gaps that are left when a diode replaces a large filler cell.
     set_db route_antenna_diode_insertion true
     route_design
     connect_global_net




Related Information
     Using the NanoRoute Router
     Calculating and Fixing Process Antenna Violations chapter of LEF/DEF Language Reference


Creating RC Model Data in TQuantus Model File
You can create and store RC model data in a TQuantus model file, which is generated by the TQuantus extraction engine. The TQuantus extraction
engine is an advanced extraction engine that is enabled by default for postRoute effort level medium extraction. The TQuantus engine is tightly
integrated with NanoRoute and drives track assignment-based timing and SI optimization/postRoute optimization, and timing-driven routing.
The TQuantus model file generated using this engine has a benefit over the Quantus techfile in that it provides a simplified RC model data for
implementation as compared to the Quantus techfile that has a complicated RC table. However, there is a small tradeoff for accuracy and runtime. You
can create the TQuantus model file by using the create_tquantus_model_file command inside the Innovus environment.




Last Updated in September 2025                                            992                                                  Product Version 23.16
                                                Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                    Design Implementation Capabilities--Using the NanoRoute Router



Before creating the TQuantus model file, ensure that the following are available in the Innovus database:
     QRC techfiles
     Pitch information from the technology LEF file
     RC corners




Last Updated in September 2025                                           993                                Product Version 23.16
                                                Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                    Design Implementation Capabilities--Using the NanoRoute Router



Use model for TQuantus Model File
The use model for the creation and usage of TQuantus model file is detailed below:
  1. Generate the TQuantus model file using the create_tquantus_model_file -out_file command.
  2. Once the TQuantus model file is available, run the following command to bring it into the Innovus environment:
     set_db extract_rc_engine post_route
     set_db extract_rc_effort_level medium
     set_db extract_rc_tquantus_model_file tQuantus_model_file

  3. Use the check_tquantus_model_file command to check the consistency of the TQuantus model file against the various input files listed above
     inside the Innovus database. The following commands automatically invoke the check_tQuantus_model_file command to check the TQuantus
     file, if TQuantus is chosen as the extraction engine.
        a. route_design -track_opt
        b. time_design
        c. opt_design –post_route
        d. extract_rc

    Note: The software issues warnings if inconsistencies are detected in the RC corner information, Quantus techfile, or pitch information in the LEF
    technology file. If such warnings are issued, the TQuantus model file will be generated automatically.
To use the TQuantus model file during track assignment-based timing and SI optimization (TA-Opt) and opt_design –post_route optimization, specify
the -track_opt option of the route_design command. Specifying this parameter ensures that TQuantus model file is used during time_design and
opt_design -post_route optimization.
route_design –track_opt

Note: Specify the -ideal_clock parameter along with the -track_opt parameter to enable the ideal clock mode for optimization.
route_design -track_opt -ideal_clock

Note: The TQuantus flow is enabled by default. In this mode, the software instructs time_design and opt_design -post_route to use the
TQuantusModel file for optimization. You can specify a file name for the TQuantus model file by using
the extract_rc_tquantus_model_file attribute of the set_db command. If this file is not specified, it is generated automatically by the software.


Example1: Use Model for Track Assignment-Based Timing and SI Optimization
The example below details the use model for TA-Opt:
create_tquantus_model_file -out_file tQuantus_model.bin
set_db extract_rc_engine post_route
set_db extract_rc_effort_level medium
set_db extract_rc_tquantus_model_file tQuantus_model.bin

set_db delaycal_enable_si true
set_db timing_analysis_type ocv
set_db timing_analysis_cppr both
route_design –track_opt

Note: When route_design –track_opt is specified, the set_db delaycal_enable_si attribute must be set to true. If it is set to false, the software will
error out. Also, you can enable the ideal clock mode for optimization by specifying the -ideal_clock parameter along with the -track_opt parameter.


Example2: Use Model for opt_design -post_route and time_design -post_route Optimization
The example below details the use model for optDesign -postRoute and time_design -post_route optimization:
create_tquantus_model_file -out_file tQuantus_model.bin
set_db extract_rc_engine post_route
set_db extract_rc_effort_level medium
set_db extract_rc_tquantus_model_file tQuantus_model.bin
set_db delaycal_enable_si true
set_db timing_analysis_type ocv
set_db timing_analysis_cppr both
opt_design -post_route/time_design -post_route

                                                  Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                      Design Implementation Capabilities--Using the NanoRoute Router

     Using the NanoRoute Router




Support for High Frequency Routing
The NanoRoute High Frequency Router (NRHF) has been implemented to address the custom and high frequency routing needs of mixed-signal
users. It is built on NanoRoute infrastructure for full interoperability. This capability enables routing of high frequency digital signals but requires
special constraints to achieve critical performance. Before using the NanoRoute high frequency router, you must make sure that the design has routing
constraints.
The routing constraints can be specified though any of the following:
     Integration Constraint Editor form in Innovus
     Constraint Manager in Virtuoso
     Note: For constraints entered in Virtuoso, OA based flow should be used. The multiple entry mechanism for routing constraints is supported to
     take additional benefit of being fully interoperable between Innovus and Virtuoso.
     set_integration_route_constraint command

The NanoRoute high frequency router can be invoked before or after placement of standard cells and blocks. All nets which have the constraints will
be routed by default. To route specific nets you can use the set_db route_selected_net_only true command.
Note: Partially routed nets will not be routed.
The following route_design command parameter supports high frequency routing:
     -high_freq
     Runs high frequency routing. It routes high frequency digital signals to achieve critical performance.
The following route Category Attributes support high frequency routing:
     route_high_freq_constraint_groups {order net match bus pair shield}
     Routes only the specified constraint groups. The constraint groups can be net, match, bus, pair, or shield.
     Note: You can add "order" in front to control the route order. If order is present, the NanoRoute high frequency router will honor the order, if not,
     the router will determine the order.
     route_high_freq_match_report_file filename
     Specifies the name of the match report file.
     route_high_freq_num_reserved_layers value
     Specifies the number of metal layers to reserve for high frequency routing above the standard cell area so that routing has more resources to
     access standard cell pins.
     route_high_freq_remove_floating_shield {true|false}
     Removes floating shield segments for high frequency nets.
     route_high_freq_search_repair {true|false}
     Runs search and repair to remove violations.
     route_high_freq_search_repair value
     Specifies the shielding wire length. All shielding wires longer than the specified value are preserved by NanoRoute for high frequency nets.
Additionally, the NRHF router honors the set_route_attributes -hard_spacing_length_check settings. The -hard_spacing_length_check parameter
specifies the table of parallel run length on each layer. It relaxes a hard NDR into a soft NDR whenever the parallel run length between the NDR wire
and another wire is smaller than the specified value. Honoring this attribute enables NRHF router to ignore the NDR for a portion of the route which is
a percentage of the total wire length for areas where the number of tracks are limited. With this, NRHF can maintain default spacing for the short PG
wires and use routing resources more effectively.




Last Updated in September 2025                                             995                                                    Product Version 23.16
                                                 Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                     Design Implementation Capabilities--Using the NanoRoute Router



Related Information

     Using the NanoRoute Router
     High Frequency Router In Innovus chapter of Mixed Signal (MS) Interoperability Guide
     Integration Constraint Editor in the Innovus Menu Reference.



Using the Third-party ECO Flow
Innovus supports using third-party data for ECO routing and layer only ECO routing. It supports non-DEF connectivity models, multiple NDRs per net,
and other third-party structures in order to enable ECO routing on non-native data.
To enable third-party support, specify:
set_db route_third_party_data true

Note: Since the third-party data uses DEF data types for different purposes and is formed for a completely different use model, it is important to
translate it into Cadence types. With proper setup Innovus accepts third-party data for ECO Routing. Performing the ECO and resolving the remaining
violation functions much same as with standard Innovus data and is compatible with Cadence flows.
The recommended steps for importing third-party data into Innovus for ECO routing or post-mask layer only ECO routing are:
     Set the third-party option
     Read in the DEF file while preserving the non-native DEF connectivity shapes
     Convert the data to Innovus forms


Sample TCL Script

The following is a sample TCL script that outlines this process:
# Enable the third-party options #
set_db route_third_party_data true
set_multi_cpu_usage -local_cpu 8

# Load the data #
set init_lef_file {
  lef/tech.lef
  lef/cells.lef
  lef/macros.lef
}

set init_verilog net/design.v
init_design

# Read the DEF file while preserving the non DEF connected shapes
read_def -preserve_shape design.def

# Convert special nets to regular nets with auto NDR generation
convert_special_routes_for_eco

# Introduce ECO, eco_read_def, opt_design, add/remove term etc.
route_eco -route_only_layers 1:4



Setup Considerations
While using the third-party ECO flow, be aware of the following setup considerations:
     Conversion
     Technology File
     Floorplan




Last Updated in September 2025                                          996                                                  Product Version 23.16
                                                 Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                     Design Implementation Capabilities--Using the NanoRoute Router



Conversion

The conversion step changes DEF special nets to regular nets wires so that ECO routing can be performed. Since all special nets are not required for
ECO, you can mark any special net segment that may be excluded from the conversion as +FIXED before converting the data for ECO. This may
include power and ground nets secondary powers and clocking mesh structures. Clock mesh would be detected as a loop and would be subjected to
ecoRoute changes if not marked FIXED.
Note: Cadence recommends that you perform this process carefully as the lower layers of the clock may route from the mesh to the pins and may be
changed if included in an ECO. You must focus on the intent and scope of the ECO and the design to determine which structures need to remain
special nets and which can be converted to regular wires for ECO.
Note: NanoRoute will not change any segment marked as a special net.
Example:
The following commands set the state of Snet power_ mesh in Metal6-Metal7 to FIXED.
select_routes -nets power_mesh -via_cell {*67*}
select_routes -obj_type {wire via} -type special -nets power_mesh -layer {Metal6 Metal7}
set_selected_special_status all FIXED



Technology File (tech.lef)

If you are using the Innovus System for the first time, you may face issues with the technology file and the design setup that may require investigation.
     Load the design
     Verify the DRC Violations
The route_eco command attempts to fix any DRC violations so investigating any violation is important. The following are some potential issues and
some useful tips that enable you to fix them:
     For Post-Mask ECO, the design used should be tape-out clean and should not have any DRC violations in Innovus. If Innovus reports violations,
     it indicates that the Innovus tech.lef is a different version than the one used to tape out the design.
     DRC violations at this stage can also indicate a rule interpretation discrepancy and require support for your Cadence representative. You must
     make sure that the rule sets match. There may be differences in the Innovus tech.lef file and the tape-out rule set that need to be fixed.
     Investigate any violations to align the status before proceeding with ECO routing.
     Verify the recommended rules that may have been optionally disabled in the original tape out routing. The design rules must match and show 0
     violations before proceeding with ECO routing.


Floorplan

Check the floorplan objects like blockages, cell rows, and tracking. The cells need matching rows for the placer to work with the design and for diode
cells and fillers to be replaced. The tracking should align with the routing for the route_eco command to interact correctly with the existing routing and
cell pin access.
Note: Partial routing blockages are supported in ECO routing. However, a large number of violations in one area may indicate a blockage that should
be removed. The third-party routing blockages are generally more tool specific and should be removed.


Related Information
     Using the NanoRoute Router
     Running ECO Routing


Troubleshooting Routing
If you have problems with your design, try the following troubleshooting tips:
     Check the log file for errors and warnings. Correct the problems and continue routing or reroute, as appropriate. For example, the router might
     stop routing automatically if it finds too many violations. If the router stops unexpectedly, check the log file for a message that the router has
     reached the maximum number of violations and then set the set_db route_detail_auto_stop attribute to false to continue routing.
     Verify connectivity and geometry before and after routing and compare results. You can also use the check_connectivity command to check
     the connectivity.
     Save the database after routing and restore it in a new session in the Innovus software. Saving and restoring the database clears temporary data




Last Updated in September 2025                                             997                                                   Product Version 23.16
                                              Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                  Design Implementation Capabilities--Using the NanoRoute Router



     structures in memory.
     Issue the write_def command, then read_def, and reroute. The write_def command saves all routing information in DEF and restores a clean
     database for routing.


Related Information
     Using the NanoRoute Router
     Evaluating Violations




Last Updated in September 2025                                       998                                               Product Version 23.16
                                                 Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                     Design Implementation Capabilities--Using the NanoRoute Router



Via Pillar Flow
Via pillars are composed of stacked parallel wire segments on intermediate layers. Each of the wire segments, except in the top layer, is connected
above and below. The entire via pillar structure looks like a popular tower building game where blocks are stacked on top of each other. The
individual wire segments are sometimes called fingers. There must be exactly one finger in the top layer of the via pillars.




There are several benefits of adding via pillars in a design:
     Connecting to multiple fingers of a pin
     Replacing multi-cut via at DPT layers
     Preventing EM violations
     Improving timing performance
     Adding to the robustness of slack
     Improving utilization
Innovus provides a very flexible, easy-to-use, and effective automatic via pillar flow.


Related Information
     Automatic Via Pillar Flow
     Data Preparation for Via Pillar Insertion
     PreRoute Placement Via-Pillar Handling
     Via Pillar Insertion during Clock Tree Synthesis
     Via Pillar Insertion during Routing
     Alternate Flow: PostRoute Via Pillar Optimization
     Via Pillar Handling in ECO Flow
     Opportunistic Via Pillar Insertion
     Troubleshooting Via Pillar Insertion




Last Updated in September 2025                                              999                                              Product Version 23.16
                                                  Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                      Design Implementation Capabilities--Using the NanoRoute Router



Automatic Via Pillar Flow
The following flow chart illustrates the Innovus Via Pillar flow:




Initialize DB

To prepare for the via pillar flow, you must provide via pillar rule definitions in LEF. You also need to associate library cell pins with a list of applicable
via pillars. The details of the via pillar rule definitions and the association lists will be discussed in this document.


Via Pillar PnR Flow
Innovus provides an automatic PnR via pillar insertion flow. Innovus Via Pillar optimization has four effort modes EM-only, low, medium, and high.
Innovus GigaOpt automatically evaluates timing tradeoffs and assigns the best via pillar rules to the instance pins (not cell pins). Innovus NanoRoute
implements the via pillars based on the assigned stack via rules to instance pins or cell pins (if EM requirement exists).


PostRoute Via Pillar Sizing and Timing-Driven Eco Route
In preRoute stages, the timing of design is not as accurate. As a result, the via pillar flow faces the challenges of bridging the preRoute-postRoute
timing correlation gap. Once a postRoute database has been created, GigaOpt is able to more aggressively upsize or add via pillars based on the
more accurate postRoute timing. Furthermore, timing-driven ECO route focuses on creating optimal routing topology on the output pin to achieve
better timing.


Opportunistic Via Pillar
With a post-routed design, the Innovus NanoRoute engine can scan for available tracks around an output pin and add wire segments to either insert a
new via pillar or upgrade the current via pillar with more fingers at lower layers. Opportunistic via pillar optimization is DRC clean by construction.
There is no need to reroute the design after this optimization.




Last Updated in September 2025                                               1000                                                    Product Version 23.16
                                                 Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                     Design Implementation Capabilities--Using the NanoRoute Router

Defining Via Pillars in LEF
Via pillars are defined either in the tech LEF file or in a separate LEF file. You can use the STACKVIALAYERRULE and the STACKVIARULE rules to define
via pillars in LEF. You can use a stack via layer rule (STACKVIALAYERRULE) to define stack via rules on individual layers. These rules are combined to
form a stack via rule (STACKVIARULE).

     Note: The STACKVIALAYERRULE statements should be defined prior to STACKVIARULE in the tech LEF file.


     STACKVIALAYERRULE

     STACKVIARULE

     Sample Definition of Stack Via Layer Rule


STACKVIALAYERRULE

You can define a stack via layer rule by using the STACKVIALAYERRULE in the PROPERTYDEFINITIONS statement:
PROPERTYDEFINITIONS
LIBRARY LEF_CDN_STACKVIALAYERRULE STRING
    “STACKVIALAYERRULE stackLayerRuleName
        LAYER cutLayerName [CUTCLASS className]
           ROWCOL numCutRows numCutCols
           [XPITCH xPitch [MAXXPITCH maxXPitch]]
           [YPITCH yPitch [MAXYPITCH maxYPitch]]
           [BELOWMINLENGTH belowMinLength]
           [ABOVEMINLENGTH aboveMinLength]
           [MAXCELLEXTENSION extensionPitch]
           [OFFGRIDCUT]
           [MAXXLINEEND maxLeftExtensiion maxRightExtension]
           [MAXYLINEEND maxBottomExtensiion maxTopExtension]
           ; “ ;
END PROPERTYDEFINITIONS

where,
     ABOVEMINLENGTH aboveMinLength
     Specifies the minimum length of the default width wires on the above metal layer. This construct must be specified with XPITCH and/or YPITCH.
     BELOWMINLENGTH belowMinLength
     Specifies the minimum length of the default width wires on the below metal layer. This construct must be specified with XPITCH and/or YPITCH.
     MAXCELLEXTENSION extensionPitch
     Specifies the maximum number of pitches extensionPitch extended from the cell boundary of the pin along both directions from which the
     stacked vias must be enclosed. By default, the stacked vias must be inside the cell boundary if this construct is not specified.
     MAXXLINEEND maxLeftExtensiion maxRightExtension
     Forms a boundary box of the pin shapes to which the stacked vias connect and expands to the left and right horizontally by maxLeftExtension
     and maxRightExtension, respectively. All of the via cuts on this layer must be enclosed by this expanded boundary box. If negative values are
     given, it would shrink the boundary box in the given direction. This construct should be mutually exclusive to MAXCELLEXTENSION.
     MAXXPITCH maxXPitch
     Specifies the maximum number of pitch by which all the vertical minimum-default-width metal wires of the stacked vias could be apart. The pitch
     must be smaller than or equal to the given value.
     MAXYLINEEND maxBottomExtensiion maxTopExtension
     Forms a boundary box of the pin shapes to which the stacked vias connect and expands to the bottom and top vertically by maxBottomExtension
     and maxTopExtension, respectively. All of the via cuts on this layer must be enclosed by this expanded boundary box. If negative values are
     given, it would shrink the boundary box in the given direction. This construct should be mutually exclusive to MAXCELLEXTENSION.

     MAXYPITCH maxYPitch
     Specifies the maximum number of pitch by which all the horizontal minimum-default-width metal wires of the stacked vias could be apart. The
     pitch must be smaller than or equal to the given value.




Last Updated in September 2025                                           1003                                                  Product Version 23.16
                                                 Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                     Design Implementation Capabilities--Using the NanoRoute Router



     OFFGRIDCUT
     Specifies that the cuts could be placed off grid to block fewer routing and the below metal shape would become an L shape. It is typically applied
     on a transition cut layer in which the cut size is larger than the default wire width on the below metal.
     ROWCOL numCutRows numCutCols
     Specifies the number of cut rows and columns of a multiple-cuts via of cut class className to use on the pin. It provides the stack via row and
     column dimension. Number of vias (fingers) in y direction (ROW) and x direction (COL).

          Note: The ROWCOL configurations of adjacent STACKVIALAYERRULEs must be aligned correctly. The top via pillar layer must be
          exactly one (1) finger.


     STACKVIALAYERRULE stackLayerRuleName
     LAYER cutLayerName [CUTCLASS className]
     ROWCOL numCutRows numCutCols
     Specifies a stacked via rule on cut layer cutLayerName with className cuts. If CUTCLASS is not specified, the cut class with the smallest cut size
     would be used or the table lookup cut class would be applied based on the wire width if METALWIDTHVIAMAP is defined. These stacked via rules
     would be combined in STACKVIARULE to build stacked vias on top of some special instance pins. ROWCOL specifies the number of cut rows and
     columns of a multiple-cuts via of cut class className to use on the pin.
     XPITCH xPitch
     Specifies that all the individual cuts must be on the grid on both the top and bottom metal layers. It specifies the minimum number of tracks
     between adjacent vertical wires.
     YPITCH yPitch
     Specifies that all the individual cuts must be on the grid on both the top and bottom metal layers. It specifies the minimum number of tracks
     between adjacent horizontal wires.
     For example, if the ypitch / xpitch is 3, there should be 2 empty track between the horizontal and vertical fingers respectively.


STACKVIARULE

A stack via rule is defined to specify a list of stack via rules for individual layers.
You can define a stack via rule by using the STACKVIARULE rule in the PROPERTYDEFINITIONS statement:
PROPERTYDEFINITIONS
LIBRARY LEF_CDN_STACKVIARULE STRING
     “STACKVIARULE ruleName {stackLayerRuleName}...[EMFACTOR emFactor]
           ; “ ;
END PROPERTYDEFINITIONS

where,
     EMFACTOR emFactor
     Specifies an EM-effective factor to indicate how well this stack via rule could prevent EM violations. The higher the specified number, the more
     effective is the rule in preventing EM violations.
     STACKVIARULE ruleName {stackLayerRuleName}...
     Specifies a stacked via rule, which consists of a list of stack via rules on individual layers in the given stackLayerRuleName. This list of stack via
     rules are the rule names specified with STACKVIALAYERRULE. stackLayerRuleName should correspond to individual layer rules on
     consecutive layers.

          Note: You cannot define multiple stackLayerRuleName on the same layer.




Last Updated in September 2025                                             1004                                                    Product Version 23.16
                                                  Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                      Design Implementation Capabilities--Using the NanoRoute Router



Sample Definition of Stack Via Layer Rule

The following is a sample definition of a via pillar from M1 to M4 (with 3, 2, 2, 1 fingers in M1, M2, M3, M4, respectively) defined in the vp_rule.lef file.

PROPERTY LEF_CDN_STACKVIALAYERRULE “
     STACKVIALAYERRULE SVIA1 LAYER VIA1 CUTCLASS VSINGLECUT ROWCOL 2 3 XPITCH 2 YPITCH 3;
     STACKVIALAYERRULE SVIA2 LAYER VIA2 CUTCLASS VSINGLECUT ROWCOL 2 2 XPITCH 3 YPITCH 3;
     STACKVIALAYERRULE SVIA3 LAYER VIA3 CUTCLASS VSINGLECUT ROWCOL 1 2 XPITCH 2 YPITCH 1;“ ;
END PROPERTYDEFINITIONS

PROPERTY LEF_CDN_STACKVIARULE “
     STACKVIARULE VPPERF_M1_M4_3_2_2_1 SVIA1 SVIA2 SVIA3 ; “ ;
END PROPERTYDEFINITIONS

Preparing the Cell Pin Via Pillar Association List
As part of data preparation for via pillar insertion, you must associate the cell pins with their applicable via pillars by using the set_via_pillars or
set_db command. The set_via_pillars command sets a via pillar list for a cell term or a single via pillar for an instance term.

set_via_pillars {base_pin} via_pillar_list [-required {false | true}]

Examples of set_via_pillars command usage:
      The following command sets via pillar to cell pin AN2_NOM_D16*/Z:
      foreach cell [get_db base_cells .name AN2_NOM_D16*] {set_via_pillars -base_pin $cell/Z {VPPERF_M2_M4_1_2_1 VPPERF_M2_M5_1_2_2_1
      VPPERF_M2_M9_1_2_2_2_2_2_2_1} }

You can also use the set_db command to associate the cell pin terms with their applicable via pillars.
Example of set_db command usage:
      The following command sets via pillar to cell pin AN2_NOM_D6*/Z:
      foreach cell [get_db base_cells .name AN2_NOM_D6*] {set_db db base_pin:$cell/Z .stack_via_rule_list {VPPERF_M1_M4_1_2_2_1
      VPPERF_M1_M5_1_2_2_2_1} }

The cell pin via pillar association list can be saved in a file. For example, vp_association.tcl.
The EM via pillar is defined to be the smallest via pillar in the via pillar list that presumably satisfies the EM requirement.
The -required parameter of the set_via_pillars command specifies that an EM via pillar is required for the cell pin.
      If the -required parameter is set to false, the pin by default does not need any via pillar. In this case, associating the pin to the via pillar is
      optional.
      If the -required parameter is set to true, the pin must apply a via pillar on it. The .stack_via_required attribute of the base_pin is also updated.

     Example:
     foreach cell [get_db base_cells .name AN2_NOM_D8*] {set_via_pillars -base_pin $cell/Z -required true {VP1 VP2 VP3}}.
     OR
     foreach cell [get_db base_cells .name AN2_NOM_D8*] {
     set_db base_pin:$cell/Z .stack_via_rule_list {VP1 VP2 VP3}
     set_db base_pin:$cell/Z .stack_via_required true}

     In above example, the AN2_NOM_D8*/Z pin has three different via pillar rules to choose. After the above command is executed, for all the instances
     associated to AN2_NOM_D8*/Z in the design, their Z pin can only have the via pillar rule VP1,VP2, or VP3. When the -required parameter is set
     to true, all instances in the design that refer to AN2_NOM_D8* will automatically get a default via pillar rule. This default via pillar rule is decided by
     the tool by calculating the minimal routing resource needed among the given via pillar types.
     Here, with the -required parameter set to true, the tool will find the smallest via pillar type among VP1, VP2, and VP3 and assign the smallest
     via pillar to all AN2_NOM_D8*/Z pins. if the design gets modified and new instances are added, the new AN2_NOM_D8*/Z pins will also get associated
     with the default via pillar rule.
To query the existing cell-pin via pillar setting, you can use the get_via_pillars command or get_db command:
get_via_pillars –base_pin AN2_NOM_D8 /Z

OR
get_db base_pin:AN2_NOM_D8/Z .stack_via_rule_list

Earlier, we only used the set_db command to associate secondary PG pins:
set_db base_pin:HDR27XSICWD1BWP300H11P57PDLVT/TVDD .stack_via_rule_list VPEM_M1_M4_1_4_3_1
set_db base_pin:HDR27XSICWD1BWP300H11P57PDLVT/TVDD .stack_via_required true


      Now the set_via_pillars command can also be used to associate secondary PG pins.
      For example, the following command sets the via pillars on secondary PG pins:
      set_via_pillars -base_pin HDR27XSICWD1BWP300H11P57PDLVT/TVDD VPEM_M1_M4_1_4_3_1 -required true

      As the TVDD pin usually will have a multiple M1 shape style, you must ensure that this pin has the MUSTJOINALLPORTS property defined in the
      LEF and ensure that set_db read_physical_must_join_all_ports true is set.
      This is default in 21.1x.




Last Updated in September 2025                                               1006                                                     Product Version 23.16
                                                Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide

Associating Via Pillar Type for Design Instance/Pins
The get_via_pillars command can be used to query for the current via pillar type associated with an instance/pin. For example, the following
command returns the existing cell-pin via pillar setting for inst1/Z:
get_via_pillars –instTerm inst1/Z
The set_via_pillars command can be used to overwrite or update the current via pillar type association for an instance/pin. For example, the
following command sets a via pillar type to inst1/Z on an instTerm:
set_via_pillars –instTerm inst1/Z VP2


     Note: Only a single via pillar can be specified with the -instterm parameter whereas a via pillar list is allowed with the -term. parameter.
     Note: The single via pillar specified for the -instterm parameter must be on the -term via pillar list of the corresponding cell term.
     Note: The set_via_pillars command can also be used to clear/unset the current via pillar type association on an instance term using empty
     string (“”).

The -required parameter sets the .stackViaRequied attribute for the term/instTerm values specified by the -term or -instTerm parameters. Setting
the .stackViaRequied attribute determines whether the stack vias are required for the specified term. To enable the instance term via pillar rule you
must set either the -required parameter to 1 on the cell term or specify the via pillar on the instance term with the -required parameter set to 1. This
step is important for the via pillar association to take effect on the instance term When cell term (-base_pin) is set with -required 1 but the instance
term (-pin) is empty, the Innovus router will pick the smallest via pillar rule in the cell term list to save the routing resources.

     Note: With the -required parameter is set to 1, the specified -instterm via pillar is always implemented by the router.

Example:
     The following command associates via pillar type, ViaPillar1, to the instance term inst/Z:
     set_via_pillars -instterm $inst/Z -required 1 ViaPillar1

     Now, since the single via pillar specified for the -instterm parameter must be on the -term via pillar list of the corresponding cell term, the
     following command must be specified
     set_via_pillars -term $cell/Z {ViaPillar1 ViaPillar2 ViaPillar3 ViaPillar4}

     The following command clears the current via pillar association from the instance term inst/Z:
     set_via_pillars -instterm $inst/Z -required 1 “”

The get_via_pillars command or the get_db command can be used to query for the current via pillar type associated with an instance/pin. For
example, the following command returns the existing cell-pin via pillar setting for inst1/Z:
get_via_pillars –pin inst1/Z
OR
get_db pin:inst1/Z .stack_via_rule_list

The set_via_pillars command or the set_db command be used to overwrite or update the current via pillar type association for an instance/pin. For
example, the following command sets a via pillar type to inst1/Z on an instance pin:
set_via_pillars –pin inst1/Z VP2
OR
set_db pin:inst1/Z .stack_via_rule_list VP2


     Note: Only a single via pillar can be specified with the -pin parameter whereas a via pillar list is allowed with the -base_pin parameter using
     the set_via_pillars command.
     Note: The single via pillar specified for the -pin parameter must be on the -base_pin via pillar list of the corresponding cell term.
     Note: The set_via_pillars command can also be used to clear/unset the current via pillar type association on an instance term using empty
     string (“”).

The -required parameter sets the .stack_via_requied attribute for the base_pin/pin values specified by the -base_pin or -pin parameters. Setting the
.stack_via_required attribute determines whether the stack vias are required for the specified term. To enable the instance term via pillar rule you
must either set the -required parameter to true on the cell term or specify the via pillar on the instance term with -required parameter to true. This step is
important for the via pillar association to take effect on the instance term. When cell term (-base_pin) is set with -required true but the instance term
(-pin) is empty, the Innovus router will pick the smallest via pillar rule in the cell term list to save the routing resources.




Last Updated in September 2025                                              1008                                                    Product Version 23.16
                                                 Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                     Design Implementation Capabilities--Using the NanoRoute Router



    Note: With the -required parameter is set to true, the specified -pin via pillar is always implemented by the router.


Example:
     Either of the following commands associate via pillar type, ViaPillar1, to the instance term inst/Z:
     set_via_pillars -pin $inst/Z -required true ViaPillar1
     OR
     set_db pin:inst1/Z .stack_via_rule_list ViaPillar1
     set_db pin:inst1/Z .stack_via_required true

     Now, since the single via pillar specified for the -pin parameter must be on the -base_pin via pillar list of the corresponding cell term, the
     following command(s) must already be specified.
     set_via_pillars -base_pin $cell/Z {ViaPillar1 ViaPillar2 ViaPillar3 ViaPillar4}
     OR
     set_db base_pin:$cell/Z .stack_via_rule_list {ViaPillar1 ViaPillar2 ViaPillar3 ViaPillar4}

     The following command clears the current via pillar association from the instance term inst/Z:
     set_via_pillars -pin $inst/Z -required true “”
     OR
     set_db pin:inst1/Z .stack_via_rule_list “”




Related Information
     Via Pillar Flow

Preparing the Design Data

Design data must be prepared properly for the via pillar flow to work in Innovus. Following are some guidelines that must be followed to ensure the
correct generation of vias:
     If the library cell pins have multiple fingers and must be connected (MUSTJOINALLPORTS rule has been defined in LEF), then use
     the read_physical_must_join_all_ports attribute of the read_physical Category Attributes.
     read_physical_must_join_all_ports {true | false}
     If the read_physical_must_join_all_ports attribute is set to false, the LEF_MUSTJOINALLPORTS property on a LEF PIN auto-creates
     MUSTJOIN pins for each PORT. If set to true, the MUSTJOIN pins are not created, but the router must connect to every port with a signal wire or
     signal via that physically overlaps all the ports.
     For example:
     set_db read_physical_must_join_all_ports true


           Note: Even though the read_physical_must_join_all_ports attribute is enabled by default in 21.1x, it is recommended that you set it or
           check its value.


           Note: If the read_physical_must_join_all_ports attribute is required, you must make sure that it is set before any library cell LEF file is
           parsed in Innovus. It must be set before the init_design or read_db command. Otherwise, inconsistency in DB might cause unexpected
           issues down the flow.


     If you use the init_design command to initialize the design data, it is recommended that you include the via pillar LEF in the list of LEF files to
     be imported by the init_lef_files attribute before calling the init_design command.
     set_db add_route_vias_auto true
     set_db add_route_vias_advanced_rule
     ...
     set_db init_verilog $netlist
     set_db init_lef_files “tlef.lef vp_rule.lef std.lef”
     ...
     init_design
     source vp_association.tcl


           Note: The following add_route_vias Category Attributes are required before the init_design command is called. It is default in 19.2x
           onwards for 32nm process nodes and below.
           set_db add_route_vias_auto true
           set_db add_route_vias_advanced_rule


     From a saved Innovus design, you can add the stack via rules by including the LEF file in the .globals file in the saved design directory:
     # in .globals
     ...
     set_db init_lef_file “tlef.lef vp_rule.lef std.lef”
     ...

     You can also use the read_physical -add_lefs command after using the read_db command to read in the via pillar LEF file:
     # start from a db without via pillar LEF
     read_db db/design.enc.dat design
     read_physical -add_lefs vp_rule.lef
     add_route_via_defs -advanced_rule
     ...

     If you choose to use the read_physical -add_lefs command after the read_db command, then the add_route_via_defs command must be
     called explicitly and immediately after the read_physical -add_lefs command.
     Any Innovus commands, when invoked between the restore command, read_db, and before reading the via pillar lef and generating the NR
     stack vias (read_physical -add_lefs and add_route_via_defs -advanced_rule) commands, may prevent the vias from being generated




Last Updated in September 2025                                            1010                                                   Product Version 23.16
                                                Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                    Design Implementation Capabilities--Using the NanoRoute Router



     correctly.

    Note: Cadence recommends loading all the LEF files including via pillar definitions at the beginning of the flow.

Understanding the Report

In the preRoute stage, you will find the following report in the place_opt_design stage of the flow.
The report shows the number of instance pins that have stack via rules assigned to them.
**** Begin NDR-Layer And Stack Via Rules Usage Statistics ****
Layer 5 has 24512 constrained nets
Layer 10 has 216 constrained nets
Stack Via Rule "VPPERF_M2_M9_1_2_2_2_2_2_2_1" is asserted on 141 instance pins, 0 cell pins
Stack Via Rule "VPPERF_M2_M5_1_2_2_1" is asserted on 16 instance pins, 0 cell pins
Stack Via Rule "VPPERF_M1_M4_1_4_2_1" is asserted on 2 instance pins, 0 cell pins
Stack Via Rule "VPPERF_M1_M4_1_3_2_1" is asserted on 26 instance pins, 0 cell pins
Stack Via Rule "VPPERF_M1_M4_1_2_2_1" is asserted on 460 instance pins, 0 cell pins
Stack Via Rule "VPPERF_M2_M4_1_2_1" is asserted on 685 instance pins, 0 cell pins
**** End NDR-Layer And Stack Via Rules Usage Statistics ****


The via pillar insertion happens in the first stage of route_design command. You can find the stack via implementation ratio in the innovus.log or logv
file.
The report shows the number of instance pins that have stack via rules assigned to them.

In the above example, summation of numerators equals 1940, summation of denominators equals 1940, which means there is a 100% via pillar
insertion. There will be cases where the insertion percentage is less than 100%, this might be because of lack of routing resources.
Innovus attempts to insert smaller via pillars if the currently assigned via pillar cannot be inserted successfully. If an EM via pillar is required for the cell
pin, at least the EM via pillar will be inserted even with DRC violations. If an EM via pillar is not required, this instance pin could end up with no via
pillar at all if all via pillars of the cell pin fail to be inserted. We give a statistics table at the end of via pillar insertion like shown above if a via pillar is
added/removed/downgraded/upgraded. Detailed failure log can be found in the routing logv file.

Understanding the Stack Via Rule Constraints Report

The report_route_type_constraints command is used to report the route type constraints that are being added on nets during the Innovus flow. After
the design is routed, the -stack_via_rule parameter of the report_route_type_constraints command can be used to report the pins with stack via
rule constraints. It reports detailed stack via rule constraints information.
For each stack via rule constraint, the following summaries are reported:
     Routing cost
     Number of constraints applied
     Number of constraints implemented
     Adherence ratio
     Bottom-layer constraints
     List of cells on which the stack via rule constraint was applied on driver
A sample stack via rule constraints report is shown below:




The list of cells on which a stack via rule constraint was applied on driver is also reported:

Advanced Usage
If you are experienced with the via pillar flow you may try the following advanced usage features:
     Removing Via Pillar Associations
     Defining a Keepout Box




Removing Via Pillar Associations

You can use the following commands to remove via pillar associations on all library cell pins or all on associated instance terms:

 Command                                             Action

 set_db base_pins .stack_via_required false          Removes via pillar list of all libCells
 set_db base_pins .stack_via_rule_list {}

 set_db insts.pins.stack_via_rule “”                 Removes all instTerms via pillar settings




Defining a Keepout Box

For some designs, it is helpful to specify a keepout box for some cells or instances so that there is more space to have via pillars inserted. You can
use the create_pg_keep_out command to define the PG keepout box for a cell or a pin. PG keepouts can be applied on the cell or cell pin. Keepout
values can be positive or negative and are always from the edge for both pin and cell.




For example, the following command keeps mycustomcell/the_m4_pin away from M5 power stripes:
create_pg_keep_out -cell mycustomcell -pin the_m4_pin -layer M5

PreRoute Placement Via-Pillar Handling
The Innovus Placement engine and the Early Global Route engine can recognize the via pillar rule requirements and accordingly do the necessary
adjustments for allocating resources for them.


Dense PG Via Pillar Aware Placement
The Innovus placement engine calculates the routing track needed by each via pillar rule for each instance and the considers the existing preRoute
Power/Ground structure.




As shown in the above illustration, the Innovus placement engine checks the existing preRoute wires and finds the available track for each via layer to
decide if the current location is legal for the given via pillar rule. If the resource in the current location cannot fulfill the rule requirement of the via pillar,
it refines the location of the instance to ensure a successful via pillar insertion.

     Tip: You do not need to specify any additional options to enable this placement feature. The placement engine will automatically handle it once
     it detects the via pillar association in the lib/design.



Via Pillar Aware Placement
Based on the required via pillar resources, the Innovus placer automatically creates internal halos needed to avoid multiple instances for being placed
too close to each other. This helps to avoid congestion issues.




After considering the pre-Route wire in the floorplan, the same instance the same instance placed in different locations may have different halos.




Last Updated in September 2025                                                 1017                                                       Product Version 23.16
                                                 Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                     Design Implementation Capabilities--Using the NanoRoute Router

Via Pillar Insertion during Clock Tree Synthesis
After clock trees are synthesized, it is often necessary to use via pillars for electro migration (EM) prevention or timing improvement. You can use the
set_ccopt_property command to specify and overwrite the via pillar settings for the instances and nets in the clock trees.

To set a property, you need to specify the property name, value, object type, and object pattern. The following syntax are acceptable:
  1. set_ccopt_property <name> <value> [-obj <obj_pattern>]
  2. set_ccopt_property <name> [-obj        <obj_pattern>] <value>

  3. set_ccopt_property [-obj <obj_pattern>] <name> <value>

Where,
name is the name of the property to be set. For via pillars, can use the stack_via_rule property.
value is the new value to which you want to set the property. For via pillars, can specify the name of the via_pillar_rule to use.


     Note: Only one via pillar rule can be specified using the set_ccopt_property command.

set_ccopt_property stack_via_rule [-net_type leaf | trunk | top] [-clock_tree clock_tree_name] \
 [-lib_pin pin_name] via_pillar_rule

The options of the set_ccopt_property command can be mixed to provide a complex configuration of via pillar settings for the clock trees in the
design.
Example:
     The following command sets the CTS trunk nets to use via pillar M1M4_1332:
     set_ccopt_property stack_via_rule –net_type trunk M1M4_1332

     The following command makes all nets in clock tree, ck1, use via pillar M1M4_1332:
     set_ccopt_property stack_via_rule –clock_tree ck1 M1M4_1332

     The following command sets all cell pins X/Y in clock trees to use via pillar M1M4_1332
     set_ccopt_property stack_via_rule –lib_pin X/Y M1M4_1332


     Note: The values set by the set_ccopt_property command must be consistent with the cell pin via pillar association lists specified by the
     set_via_pillars command. Any inconsistency between the values set by the set_ccopt_property and set_via_pillars commands can result
     in non-implementation of via pillars.



Sample Via Pillar Usage in Clock Tree
The following is an example of how to set up a CTS flow using via pillars:




create_route_type –name 3W2S –non_default_rule 3W2S
create_route_type –name 2W2S –non_default_rule 2W2S
create_route_type –name 1W2S –non_default_rule 1W2S




Last Updated in September 2025                                            1019                                                   Product Version 23.16
                                                 Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                     Design Implementation Capabilities--Using the NanoRoute Router



set_ccopt_property buffer_cells {BUFD8 BUFD10 BUFD12}

# For top tree:
set_ccopt_property route_type 3W2S –net_type top
set_ccopt_property stack_via_rule –lib_pin BUFD12/Z –net_type top M1_M5_43321
set_ccopt_property stack_via_rule –lib_pin BUFD10/Z –net_type top M1_M5_43321
set_ccopt_property stack_via_rule –lib_pin BUFD8/Z –net_type top M1_M5_33321

# For trunk tree:
set_ccopt_property route_type 2W2S –net_type top
set_ccopt_property stack_via_rule –lib_pin BUFD12/Z –net_type top M1_M4_4321
set_ccopt_property stack_via_rule –lib_pin BUFD10/Z –net_type top M1_M4_4321

set_ccopt_property stack_via_rule –lib_pin BUFD8/Z –net_type top M1_M4_3221

# For leaf tree:
set_ccopt_property route_type 1W2S –net_type leaf
set_ccopt_property stack_via_rule –lib_pin BUFD12/Z –net_type top M1_M4_4321


     Note: Even if the user did not specify any rule for clock buffers, CTS will still honor the minimal EM via pillar requirement.

After clock trees are synthesized, it is often necessary to use via pillars for electromigration (EM) prevention or timing improvement. You can use the
set_db cts_stack_via_rule [_top/trunk/leaf] command to specify and overwrite the via pillar settings for the instances and nets in the clock trees.

set_db cts_stack_via_rule [_top/trunk/leaf]
via_pillar_rule [-index {clock_tree <clock_tree name>}]

set_db base_pin:<base pin> .cts_stack_via_rule[_top/trunk/leaf]
via_pillar_rule [-index {clock_tree <clock_tree name>}]


     Note: Only one via pillar rule can be specified using the set_db cts_stack_via_rule [_top/trunk/leaf]command.

The cts_stack_via_rule attribute can be set globally or set to certain base_pin.
The -index option can be mixed to provide a complex configuration of via pillar settings for the clock trees in the design.
Example:
     The following command sets the CTS trunk nets to use via pillar M1M4_1332:
     set_db cts_stack_via_rule_trunk M1M4_1332

     The following command makes all nets in clock tree, ck1, use via pillar M1M4_1332:
     set_db cts_stack_via_rule –M1M4_1332 -index {clock_tree ck1}

     The following command sets all cell pins X/Y in clock trees to use via pillar M1M4_1332
     set_db base_pin:X/Y .cts_stack_via_rule M1M4_1332


     Note: The values set by the “set_db base_pin:<base pin> .cts_stack_via_rule” command must be consistent with the cell pin via pillar
     association lists specified by the “set_db base_pin:<base pin> .stack_via_rule_list” command. Any inconsistency between the values set
     by the set_db base_pin:<base pin> .cts_stack_via_rule and set_db base_pin:<base pin> .stack_via_rule_list commands can result in
     non-implementation of via pillars.



Sample Via Pillar Usage in Clock Tree
The following is an example of how to set up a CTS flow using via pillars:




Last Updated in September 2025                                             1020                                                   Product Version 23.16
                                                 Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                     Design Implementation Capabilities--Using the NanoRoute Router




create_route_type –name 3W2S -route_rule 3W2S
create_route_type –name 2W2S –route_rule 2W2S
create_route_type –name 1W2S –route_rule 1W2S
set_db cts_buffer_cells {BUFD4 BUFD10 BUFD12}

# For top tree:
set_db cts_route_type_top 3W2S
set_db base_pin:BUFD12/Z .cts_stack_via_rule_top M1_M5_43321
set_db base_pin:BUFD10/Z .cts_stack_via_rule_top M1_M5_43321
set_db base_pin:BUFD8/Z .cts_stack_via_rule_top M1_M5_32221

# For trunk tree:
set_db cts_route_type_trunk 2W2S
set_db base_pin:BUFD12/Z .cts_stack_via_rule_trunk M1_M4_4321
set_db base_pin:BUFD10/Z .cts_stack_via_rule_trunk M1_M4_4321
set_db base_pin:BUFD8/Z .cts_stack_via_rule_trunk M1_M4_3221

# For leaf tree:
set_db cts_route_type_leaf 1W2S
set_db base_pin:BUFD12/Z .cts_stack_via_leaf M1_M4_4321


     Note: Even if the user did not specify any rule for clock buffers, CTS will still honor the minimal EM via pillar requirement.

Via Pillar Insertion during Routing
      Via Pillar Connection to Regular Routing
      Via Pillar with Layer Promotion

Via Pillar Connection to Regular Routing
After the actual via pillar structure is inserted in the routing stage, the router treats the top layer of the via pillar structure as the new connecting point of
the PIN. There is no side connection from the middle layer of the via pillar.
The following image shows that the router taps to the top of the via pillar (dark yellow) using a planar connection (pink).




Via Pillar with Layer Promotion


During routing, the main purpose of via pillar structure is to help the connecting net to escape from the lower high resistance layer. When a net has a
via pillar requirement, it usually means that the net with the via pillar should be assigned the preferred lowest routing layer (set_route_attributes -
bottom_preferred_routing_layer) equal to or greater than the top layer of the via pillar rule.

For example:
set_route_attributes -nets $net -bottom_preferred_routing_layer $layer_value

When the gigaOpt optimization engine inserts a via pillar property to a pin, it always specifies the -bottom_preferred_routing_layer parameter
setting for the net to promote the routing of the net to a higher layer.

     Note: If you manually insert the via pillar property to a pin, you must also set the -bottom_preferred_routing_layer parameter to keep the net
     routing on top of certain layers. If a net has via pillar property defined but not the the -bottom_preferred_routing_layer parameter, then the
     routing of the net will start from the top of the via pillar structure buy may not stay on higher layers if there is congestion around the routing area.




Last Updated in September 2025                                                1022                                                      Product Version 23.16
                                                  Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                      Design Implementation Capabilities--Using the NanoRoute Router



Via Pillar with NDR
If a net has both via pillar property and NDR rule specified, the Innovus router honors both rules. In layers where the via pillar structure is covered, the
router gives precedence to via pillar settings over the NDR rule. Thereafter, starting from the top layer of the via pillar, the NRD rule is implemented.
For example, consider a net where the NDR rule defines double width from M5~M11 and the via pillar rule defines 1X2 via from M1~M7. When the
NDR is applied to the net and its output pin will also have the via pillar property. During the routing stage, for output pin from M1~M6, the router will
use via pillar rule to construct the routing. Subsequently, starting from M7 (top layer of the via pillar) till M11 (M7~M11) it will use double width for
routing.
Since the input pin here does not have any via pillar property defined, it will follow the NDR requirement to put double width in M1~M6 as well.




Via Pillar with Routing Ordering Control
The routing order of the via pillar net can be controlled by the route_selected_net_only attribute of the route category. It specifies whether
NanoRoute routes all nets at once or routes selected nets only. To insert a via pillar on a net group, select the net and then specify the following
command:
set_db route_selected_net_only true

The route_design command will insert the via pillar on the selected nets only.
For example, during the CTS stage, the clock net via pillars get inserted before regular signal nets.




Via Pillar with Existing PreRoute Nets
Via pillar insertion cooperates with existing pre-route wires. If the preferred via pillar location is already occupied by other pre-route wires, the router
adjusts the via pillar structure to create a DRC free via pillar routing result. This process is context driven. The same via pillar rule can generate a lot of
variations of the via pillar shape for individual instance pins in the design.
If pre-route nets are movable (not fixed or skipped) at the routing stage, then via pillars are allowed to create DRC with them. The pre-routed nets can
later be re-routed in the GRDR stage.
For example, the following image shows routing results that the via pillar shapes in M3 wraparound existing PG to create a DRC-free routing structure.




Via Pillar on MUSTJOIN Ports
Logical pins can have multiple ports, which in turn can have multiple shapes. If the shapes belong to the same port, the router will connect only to
one. You can use the 'MUSTJOIN all ports' rule to specify that all ports must be connected individually. The 'MUSTJOIN all ports' rule is created by
using the following property definition for a pin :
PROPERTY LEF_CDN_MUSTJOINALLPORTS “MUSTJOINALLPORTS ; “ ;

The MUSTJOINALLPORTS LEF rule specifies that all the ports must be connected individually. If there are multiple shapes per ports, at least one of the
shapes of the port needs to be connected.
In the traditional routing approach, the router treats the MUSTJOIN ports as separate connecting points. They are connected like individual pins using




Last Updated in September 2025                                              1023                                                     Product Version 23.16
                                                 Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                     Design Implementation Capabilities--Using the NanoRoute Router



regular routing pattern. The issue with this method is that it may create signal EM concerns because the current distribution among the pin shapes
might be different.




Innovus now supports a new MUSTJOIN connection method that uses via pillar like via cells to connect the MUSTJOIN ports. One single wire is used
to connect all MUSTJOIN ports together.




To enable this feature, you must specify the following global variable before importing the design (before using the init_Design or read_db
commands), this is default in 21.1x onwards::
set_db read_physical_must_join_all_ports true


     Note: Once these settings are saved using the write_db command, you need not specify them again.

For more information, see Preparing the Design Data.




Via Pillar on Input Pin

Via pillars are inserted on output pins whereas gigaOpt optimization inserts on output pin only.
     To manually insert the via pillar property on an input pin use the set_via_pillars or set_db commands.
     To query for the via pillar property on a pin, use the get_via_pillars or get_db command.




Via Pillar on Floating Output
For spare cells that have the MUSTJOIN property defined on the output pin, you can pre-insert the MUSTJOIN connection or even pre-insert some via
pillar structure on the spare cell output pin for ECO usage at a later stage. To make the router insert a MUSTJOIN connection or a via pillar structure
on the spare cell output pin, the instance pin must have a 1-pin net attached to it. This usage model allows you to choose the individual spare cell pins
for which you want to pre-insert the MUSTJOIN connection or via pillar structure.
For example, you can put a 1-pin net on spare cells of models that have a high possibility of doing ECO in the post-mask stage to minimalize the post-
mask ECO route impact. It is best not to put a1-pin net on timing critical models to have minimum routing impact on timing.




Last Updated in September 2025                                           1024                                                   Product Version 23.16
                                                   Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                       Design Implementation Capabilities--Using the NanoRoute Router




Routing Behavior when Via Pillar Insertion Fails
During the routing stage, the router tries its best to implement the via pillar requirements, however, if it does not find a DRC free solution then it has
does the following:
      If the router finds smaller via pillar rules defined in the master cell via pillar list of the instance, then it considers inserting the smaller rule for the
      pin instead. If the trial insertion is not successful, then it keeps stepping down to other smaller rules.
      If rule being targeted is already the smallest via pillar rule in the via pillar list of the pin, then the router checks if the stack_via_required property
      of the pin is true or false. When stack_via_required property is set to true, the router goes ahead with the insertion and leaves the DRC
      violations in the routing result for you to review.
      If the pin has MUSTJOINALLPORTS property defined in the LEF and the stack_via_required property set to false, the router only does the
      must_join_all_port pin connection.

The details of the action taken by the router for each pin are recorded in the *.logv file. Following is a sample of such a log file:
###+-------Inst--------------------------+--Pin--+------------SSV------------+----Status----+
### ustg/FE_OCPC136407_FE_OFN43399_n4371         Z    VPPERF_M1_M5_3_2_4_2_1_V           Failed
###                                                    VPPERF_M1_M5_3_2_3_2_1_V           Failed
###                                                    VPPERF_M1_M4_3_2_4_1_V             Failed
###                                                    VPPERF_M1_M4_3_2_3_1_V             Failed
###                                                    VPEM_M1_M4_3_2_2_1                 Success

When the router steps down the via pillar insertion to a smaller rule, it also updates stack_via_rule property of the instance pin in DB (and the
effectiveStackVia property).

To get a report of the via pillar implementation status of the current design, use the following command:
report_route_type_constraints -detailed -stack_via_rule_only

Alternate Flow: PostRoute Via Pillar Optimization
Innovus provides an alternate flow in which via pillar optimization is applied on a routed or postroute optimized design that either has only EM via
pillars inserted or no via pillars inserted at all in the preRoute stages. The benefits of using this alternate flow include stability, improved timing, and
less changes to the existing flow.

     Note: Even while using this alternate postRoute via pillar optimization flow, the data preparation step remains critical. The design data must be
     prepared properly by using all the guidelines. For more information, see Data Preparation for Via Pillar Insertion. The LEF file that contains via
     pillar rules and the via pillar association file (library cell pins vs. via pillar lists) should also be sourced into Innovus.




During postroute optimization, via pillars are added or upsized and timing-driven routing is used to create an optimal routing topology that will improve
timing. To enable this feature and specify the effort-level for via pillar optimization you must set the -opt_via_pillar_effort attribute of
the Opt category command to medium.
set_db opt_via_pillar_effort medium


     Cadence recommends that when the design requires EM via pillars, via pillar optimization should be started from the place_opt_design stage
     (placement and preCTS optimization). Alternatively, if the design does not require EM via pillars, it is recommended that the via pillar
     optimization should be started at the postRoute stage. This will enable you to get more accurate timing, leading to more stable and definite
     timing improvements.

The opt_via_pillar_effort attribute controls the preroute and postroute optimization engine behavior for the via pillar optimization feature.
set_db opt_via_pillar_effort [none | low| medium | high ]

      -none: No via pillar insertion or upgradation is done during timing optimization process but the stackViaRequired property is always honored.

      -low: Via pillar insertion and upgradation is done only when WNS path gets stuck. This is the default behavior.

      -medium: Via pillar insertion and upgradation is considered during TNS optimization.

      -high: Via pillar insertion and upgradation is considered during the initial global optimization stage.

When the via pillar effort increases, one would expect more via pillars to be inserted in the design. However, this does not guarantee better timing
results in the end. The reason is that even though additional nets get the via pillar property they can further be improved through techniques like sizing
or buffering. Increasing the via pillar effort can lead to lower design utilization.
When the gigaOpt engine inserts or upgrades the via pillar property of instance pins, it follows the same DB rules as via pillar flow.
Note: To query for the via pillar property on a pin, use the get_via_pillars or get_db command.
In the gigaOpt log file, you can find the via pillar insertion report like below:
…
Stack Via Rule "VPPERF_M1_M4_2_1_2_1_V" is asserted on 0 instance pins, 35 cell pins
Stack Via Rule "VPEM_M1_M4_3_2_2_1" is asserted on 0 instance pins, 14 cell pins




Last Updated in September 2025                                                1026                                                   Product Version 23.16
                                                 Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                     Design Implementation Capabilities--Using the NanoRoute Router



Stack Via Rule "VPEM_M1_M4_3_1_2_1" is asserted on 0 instance pins, 9 cell pins
Stack Via Rule "VPEM_M1_M4_2_2_2_1_V" is asserted on 3 instance pins, 0 cell pins

Where,
     The cell pins number means the rules are from the stackViaRequired property of the library cell (using “-required true” in set_via_pillars –
     base_pin association). It is mainly from library requirement but not mainly for timing purpose.

     The instance pins number means that it has been inserted or upgraded by the tool for timing purpose.

Via Pillar Handling in ECO Flow
In postRoute or ECO routing stage, the route_eco command checks the existing routing with the DB stack_via_rule property. If any mismatch is found
on a pin, the route_eco command removes the existing via pillar rule from that pin and tries to insert a new via pillar based stack_via_rule property.
In postRoute stage, you can modify the via pillar and then call the route_eco command to update the via pillar implementation. Considering via pillar
routing cost concern, it is important to limit the scope of rule changes to ensure that the design QoR remains sustainable.
If an instance gets resized, the gigaOpt engine takes care of the stack_via_rule property update. If you manually resize an instance but do not update
its via pillar rule, the route_eco command realizes that the current via pillar rule may not exist in the stack_via_rule property of the new cell. It then
automatically triggers the via pillar step-down method to find the closest available via pillar rule from the stack_via_rule property of the new cell.

Opportunistic Via Pillar Insertion
After the design is routed or postRoute optimized, the NanoRoute router looks for available tracks around the instance pin to either add a new via pillar
or upgrade the original smaller via pillar to one with more fingers.
In the following example, there is no via pillar inserted on the z pin. In this case, the NanoRoute router can find nearby available tracks to add an
additional finger and create a new via pillar, VPPERF_M2_M4_1_2_1.




The following example shows an upgrade from VPEM_M1_M4_3_1_2_1 to VPPERF_M1_M4_3_2_2_1.




After opportunistic via pillar insertion, there is no need for ECO Route; opportunistic via pillar insertion is DRC clean by construction. Once again,
make sure that the data preparation is as mentioned in Data Preparation for Via Pillar Insertion.
You can perform the opportunistic via pillar insertion by using the following command:
route_design –via_pillar_opt


     Note: Optional. You can use a different cell pin via pillar association list for opportunistic via pillar insertion compared to the list used for PnR
     flow.
