       The Main Window


Controlling Cell Blockage Visibility
If you see a spacing violation when adding or editing a via or wire, it might be caused by a cell
blockage that is not currently visible.
To see cell blockages, select the Cell Blkg option on the Routing color control display (click the
slidebar to display this option). Alternatively, you can click the All Colors button to display the Color
Preferences form, then select the Cell Blockage visibility option. In addition, depending on whether
the blockage is outside or inside a cell, you must do one of the following:
       Cell blockages outside a cell are visible by default. To control the visibility of these blockages
       for particular layers, click the Wire/Via tab of the Color Preferences form. Use the buttons in
       the fifth column, Blkage, to deselect the visibility of blockages for particular layers. By default,
       all layers are selected.
       Cell blockages within a cell are not visible by default. To see these cell blockages, click the
       Wire/Via tab of the Color Preferences form, then use the buttons in the sixth column, V Blkg, to
       select the visibility of blockages for each cut layer that you want to see. By default, all layers
       are deselected.

       Editing Wires
       The Main Window




Last Updated in September 2025                         2076                               Product Version 23.16
                                    Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 ECOs and Interactive Design Editing--Editing Wires




Parallel Editing Capability
In large designs, it can be challenging to fix all DRC violations by automatic
physical implementation tools. The traditional flow in which violations are fixed one at a time by an
engineer can consume a lot of time. To address such issues, Innovus provides the parallel editing
capability through merge DB. This capability enables multiple users to make wire editing changes
in the same database in parallel.
The parallel editing capability can be used to fix DRC violations and ECO in parallel at the sign-off
stage in the physical implementation flow.


Related Information

       Parallel Edit Flow
       Parallel Editing for DRC Corrections
       Parallel Editing for ECO Changes
       Parallel Edit File Content
       Parallel Edit File Format
       Handling Conflicts in Parallel Edit Files


Parallel Edit Flow
The parallel editing flow can enable multiple users to make physical and logic ECO changes at the
same time on different areas of a design. It can support a wide range of basic wire editing and ECO
operations, such as modifying or adding a wire or via or modifying logical connections.
This flow consists of three commands: start_parallel_edit, end_parallel_edit, and
read_parallel_edit_files. All the physical and logical information modified in the area selected
through start_parallel_edit is recorded in the related ECO file by end_parallel_edit. The
read_parallel_edit_files command is then used to load all the ECO files to complete the entire
ECO operation.

The parallel edit flow can be represented as follows:




Last Updated in September 2025                         2077                           Product Version 23.16
                                    Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 ECOs and Interactive Design Editing--Editing Wires




The steps in the parallel edit flow are detailed below:
   1. Import or restore the design in which you want to make parallel edits along with other users.
      The other users can start their own Innovus session on the same database in parallel.
   2. Initialize parallel editing by using the start_parallel_edit command.
       The start_parallel_edit command is used to define the area where you need to perform
       DRC fixing or ECO operations. The command draws a yellow square on the main window to
       indicate the edit area and saves the physical data, net attributes, via cell names, and Non-
       Default Rules (NDRs) to multiple binary files. Use the -region {x1 y1 x2 y2} parameter to
       specify the coordinates of the edit area. Specify the -area_restricted parameter to write out
       only the different objects inside or touching the specified edit area. This is a strict
       interpretation of the region, and ignores the changes made outside of the region. The other
       engineers should also initialize start_parallel_edit in their sessions separately to specify
       their own operating areas. Area overlap is not recommended because it might cause ECO
       conflicts.




Last Updated in September 2025                         2078                           Product Version 23.16
                                    Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 ECOs and Interactive Design Editing--Editing Wires




       The following example shows the report information generated by start_parallel_edit.




   3. Make DRC fixes on ECO changes in your assigned area in the design. Multiple engineers
      can work simultaneously on different areas in the design in the parallel edit mode.
   4. After all your changes are done, use the end_parallel_edit command in your session to save
       the ECO file of the edits. The end_parallel_edit command compares the data in memory
       with the original data saved in the binary files by start_parallel_edit and writes the parallel
       edit information to the specified file. The following example shows the report information
       generated by end_parallel_edit.




Last Updated in September 2025                         2079                           Product Version 23.16
                                    Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 ECOs and Interactive Design Editing--Editing Wires




       Multiple end_parallel_edit commands are allowed after one start_parallel_edit. Others
       users should also issue the end_parallel_edit command in their respective sessions after
       completing their changes. Similarly, other engineers also save their modifications in
       corresponding files after he corrections for the different areas of the chip are saved in different
       files.
   5. Load all parallel edit files by using the read_parallel_edit_files command.
       The read_parallel_edit_files command loads all the specified parallel edit files and
       implements the editing changes recorded in them. In case of any conflict in the parallel edit
       files, the tool implements changes as per the specified conflict mode and writes the conflicting
       information to a report file.


Related Information
       Parallel Editing Capability
       Parallel Editing for DRC Corrections
       Parallel Editing for ECO Changes
       Parallel Edit File Content
       Parallel Edit File Format
       Handling Conflicts in Parallel Edit Files


Parallel Editing for DRC Corrections
By using the parallel editing capability, you can divide the entire chip into different areas to enable
multiple engineers to correct DRC violations in different areas of the design at the same time. After
all the changes are done, you can apply the correction results to the entire chip. The following
example shows how DRC corrections are done in a specific area of a real chip in parallel editing
mode:
Step 1



Last Updated in September 2025                         2080                              Product Version 23.16
                                    Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 ECOs and Interactive Design Editing--Editing Wires


Step 1
Load the entire chip into Innovus and use start_parallel_edit -region {x1 y1 x2 y2} to specify
the area in which DRC correction is required.




The specified area is highlighted in the GUI at this point. The check_drc command can report all
DRC violations in the highlighted area as shown below:




Last Updated in September 2025                         2081                           Product Version 23.16
                                    Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 ECOs and Interactive Design Editing--Editing Wires




As shown above, there are 5 DRC violations in the defined area. At this point, you can fix the DRC
violations manually or through some automated method in the tool. After DRC fixing is complete,
use the check_drc command again to check whether or not the DRC violations are completely fixed.




Step 2
After the DRC violations in the specified area are completely fixed, you need to save the changes
made to the design. end_parallel_edit -out_file drc_diff will save all the changes to the
drc_diff file as shown below:




Last Updated in September 2025                         2082                           Product Version 23.16
                                    Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 ECOs and Interactive Design Editing--Editing Wires




The content of the saved drc_diff file is shown below:




Step 3
Reload the original chip, and use read_parallel_edit_files -files drc_diff to read in the
corrections from the previous step. The DRC corrections will be applied to the original chip layout
so that DRC violations will be removed from the original area as shown below.




Last Updated in September 2025                         2083                           Product Version 23.16
                                    Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 ECOs and Interactive Design Editing--Editing Wires




The process described above can also be applied to a multi-user collaboration. The only additional
step you need to do is to divide the whole chip into specific areas in the first step and assign them to
different engineers.

Parallel Edit File Format
Each entry in the parallel edit file has the following format:
<action command> <object type> <{{attribute value} ...}>

action command specifies the type of action to be carried out on the given object-attributes
combination. Three types of action commands are supported: ADD, DEL, and MODIFY.
For example:
MODIFY NET {{Attribute1 Value1} {Attribute2 Value2} ...}



Sample Parallel Edit File

Here's an extract from a sample parallel edit file:
###############################################################

# Generated by: Cadence Innovus 17.10-d111_1

# OS: Linux x86_64(Host ID noi-leenap)

# Generated on: Wed Mar 8 16:46:37 2017

# Design: design_top

###############################################################

#####################################################
### The difference for each wire/via ###
#####################################################

MODIFY WIRE {{is_patch 1} {layer M1} {mask 1} {net clk_i} {pts {10904 275220 11048
275300}} {status routed} {trimmetal_box {10752 275300 11200 275420}} {trimmetal_mask 1}
}

279140}} {status routed} {trimmetal_box {18816 279140 19264 279260}} {trimmetal_mask 1}
}

278380}} {status routed} {trimmetal_box {18816 278180 19264 278300}} {trimmetal_mask 1}

       Handling Conflicts in Parallel Edit Files


Handling Conflicts in Parallel Edit Files
During parallel editing, there is a possibility of a conflict if the same attribute of an object is changed
in different parallel edit files.
Some cases in which a conflict can occur are listed below:
       Two users edit the layer of the same wire segment.
       Two users edit the width of the same NDR rule.
       One user cuts the wire segment, and another user changes the status of the same wire.
In the following examples of parallel edits, there is no conflict:
       One user edits the layer of wire, and another user edits its mask.
       One user edits the width of an NDR rule, and another user edits its spacing.
Whenever a conflict occurs in parallel editing, the way the conflict is addressed depends on
the read_parallel_edit_files -conflict_mode setting, if specified. The two possible settings for
this parameter are:




Last Updated in September 2025                         2094                              Product Version 23.16
                                    Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 ECOs and Interactive Design Editing--Editing Wires



       only_one - In case of conflicting commands, one of the command is implemented and the
       other command is recorded in the conflict report.
       stop_all - In case of conflicting commands, the tool implements neither of the conflicting
       commands and records both of them in the conflict report.
Consider the following examples.


Example 1

Two parallel editing files, pEdit_1.txt and pEdit_2.txt, have have different definitions for the
same via cell V12_1X1:
Via Cell V12_1x1 in pEdit_1.txt




Via Cell V12_1x1 in pEdit_2.txt




Last Updated in September 2025                         2095                           Product Version 23.16
                                    Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 ECOs and Interactive Design Editing--Editing Wires




When both files are read, the tool will keep the via cell name in pEdit_1.txt and rename the via cell
name to V12_1x1_1 in pEdit_2.txt. The tool will also print the following message on the console
and record it in the conflict report file:
Warning: Rename the via cell name fromV12_1x1 to V12_1x1_1 in file pEdit_2.txt.



Example 2

Two parallel editing files, pe2 and pe3, modify the same attribute of a wire.
If the read_parallel_edit_files -conflict_mode parameter is specified and set to only_one when
reading the files, the tool implements the changes in pe2 but gives the following error:
**ERROR: (IMPDBPE-104): 2 files 'pe2:17, pe3:19' modify the same wire. Only command in
file pe2 will be executed. Following commands didn't get executed:
pe3:15
pe3:16
pe3:14
pe3:19
pe3:13
pe3:17
pe3:18




Last Updated in September 2025                         2096                           Product Version 23.16
                                    Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                                 ECOs and Interactive Design Editing--Editing Wires



If the read_parallel_edit_files -conflict mode parameter is set to stop_all, the tool does not
implement either of the conflicting commands and records both of them in the conflict report in
File_name:line_num format:

**ERROR: (IMPDBPE-105): 2 files 'pe2:17, pe3:19' modify the same wire. Following
commands didn't get executed:
pe2:16
pe2:17
pe2:18
pe2:13
pe2:14
pe2:19
pe2:15
pe3:13
pe3:17
pe3:15
pe3:16
pe3:19
pe3:18
pe3:14



Related Information

       Parallel Editing Capability
       Parallel Edit File Content
       Parallel Edit File Format

                                 Design Methodology for 3D IC with Through Silicon Via




                                                                                                       11
Design Methodology for 3D IC with Through
Silicon Via

A 3D IC system usually contains several dies connected in three dimensions. In conventional IC,
the IO pins are implemented by either bumps or bonding pads on one side of the chip. To enable
the 3D interconnection, several additional components are created on the chip. Firstly, several Re
Distributed Layers (RDL) are formed on the back side of the chip. Therefore, bumps can be placed
on both front side and back side. Secondly, the Through Silicon Via (TSV) is dropped on the silicon
substrate between the front-side metal and the back side RDL. Finally, when the dies are stacked,
the aligned bumps between them constitute the data path from one die to the other.
Scheme of 3D IC Profile




Innovus supports TSV designs. In Innovus, all the dies of the 3D ICs are divided into several tiers.
Each tier contains several dies, and each die can be flipped, rotated and with offset related to the
package. With Innovus, the designers are able to specify the multi-die system configuration (the
interconnection between dies, the related position of each die), manipulate TSVs and bumps,
perform co-design, and sync-up the interface among all the dies.
Related Information
       TSV/Bump/Back Side Metal Modeling in Innovus
       Defining Keep Out Area in Hard Macros




Last Updated in September 2025                           2099                            Product Version 23.16
                                         Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
               Design Methodology for 3D IC with Through Silicon Via--TSV/Bump/Back Side Metal Modeling in
                                                         Innovus



       3D IC Flow in Innovus
       Design Import and Export in 3D IC Designs
       Stacked IC Verilog Input
       Stack Configuration Input
       Power Connectivity Input
       Interface Synchronization and Information Exchange between Dies
       TSV and Bump Manipulation
       Feedthru Handling
       TSV and Bump Routing
       Cross Die Connectivity Verification


TSV/Bump/Back Side Metal Modeling in Innovus

Back Side Metal (MB), TSV, and Micro bump are introduced to establish 3D stacked
interconnection between dies. MB is the Redistribution Layer on the back side of the substrate. TSV
is the via which penetrates the silicon substrate. The top cap layer of TSV is usually the first normal
routing layer (1) and the bottom cap layer of TSV is MB, therefore, the back side metal and the 1st
metal layer is connected through TSV.
To connect the die with the others, some solder balls (or pillars) are placed on the top metal layer or
back side metal layer. These solder balls and the underneath metal pad are called bumps in
Innovus. The aligned bump between dies is called micro bump or landing pad. The cross die signal
or power goes to/comes from the adjacent die through the micro bump. The bump between the die
and the package substrate is called flip chip bump.
TSV and Bump Cross-Section




Last Updated in September 2025                            2100                                       Product Version 23.16
                                         Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
               Design Methodology for 3D IC with Through Silicon Via--TSV/Bump/Back Side Metal Modeling in
                                                         Innovus



TSV and Bump Cross-Section
All the physical information for back side metal, TSV, and bump is defined in the LEF file (the
version for the LEF file should be LEF 57 or later). MB is modeled as ROUTING layer before the
first normal metal, and a LEF property "BACKSIDE" is assigned to MB. TSV is model as a CUT
layer with a LEF property "TYPE TSV". Below is an example of the additional information in LEF
file for TSV and back side metal definition.
The pad metal of the bump is also described in the LEF file. In the LEF file, the bump is modeled as
a cell. The bump cell has a pin which has the same shape and layer with the pad metal of the
bump. The solder ball information is not described in the LEF. The other way to categorize the
bump is based on the bump pad layer. If a bump is on top metal layer, it could be called as front
bump, and if a bump is on back side metal layer it is back bump. Micro Bump could be either front
bump or back bump depending how the die is stacked.


Example

The statement in LEF to describe the TSV is given below:
PROPERTYDEFINITIONS

     LAYER LEF58_BACKSIDE STRING ;

     LAYER LEF58_TYPE STRING ;

 …

END PROPERTYDEFINITIONS

 LAYER MB          # Back side metal layer

 TYPE ROUTING ;

 PROPERTY LEF58_BACKSIDE "BACKSIDE ; " ;

 …

END MB



LAYER TSV          # TSV cut layer between 1 & MB

 TYPE CUT ;

 PROPERTY LEF58_TYPE "TYPE TSV ; " ;

 SPACING 20.0 LAYER OVERLAP ;

 …




Last Updated in September 2025                            2101                                       Product Version 23.16
                                         Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
               Design Methodology for 3D IC with Through Silicon Via--TSV/Bump/Back Side Metal Modeling in
                                                         Innovus



END TSV

 LAYER METAL1            # 1

 TYPE ROUTING ;

     …

END METAL1

 …

VIA VIAB1

 RESISTANCE 0.01 ;

 LAYER MB ;

         RECT -11.00 -11.00 11.00 11.00 ;

 LAYER TSV ;

         RECT -5.00 -5.00 5.00 5.00 ;

 LAYER METAL1 ;

         RECT -7.0 -7.0 7.0 7.0 ;

END VIAB1

Defining Keep Out Area in Hard Macros
You can define keep-out area constraints inside macros to avoid overlap between bumps/adjacent
die edges and these areas. Innovus will report warning if creating bumps inside the keep-out area,
and this keep-out area information can be passed to the adjacent die.
Blockage area can be specified in hard macros by creating the following layers of OBS in the macro
LEF file:
       Passivation layer in front side
       Passivation layer in back side
       Master slice layer for top die
       Master slice layer for bottom die
Use the passivation layers in the LEF file for defining bump keep-out area, and Master Slice layers
in LEF for defining die edge checking. The statement in the LEF file to define blockage for die edge
checking and bump keep-out area is given below:
Layer for back bump keep out region
LAYER BACKPASSIV
    TYPE CUT ;
    PROPERTY LEF58_TYPE "TYPE PASSIVATION ; " ;
    PROPERTY LEF58_BACKSIDE "BACKSIDE ; " ;
END BACKPASSIV

Layer for front bump keep out region
LAYER TOPPASSIV
    TYPE CUT ;
    PROPERTY LEF58_TYPE "TYPE PASSIVATION ; " ;
END TOPPASSIV

A new layer for top die edge
LAYER TOPDIE
    TYPE MASTERSLICE ;
    PROPERTY LEF58_TYPE "TYPE ABOVEDIEEDGE ; " ;
END TOPDIE

A new layer for bottom die edge
LAYER BOTTOMDIE




Last Updated in September 2025                            2103                                        Product Version 23.16
                                         Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
               Design Methodology for 3D IC with Through Silicon Via--Defining Keep Out Area in Hard Macros



    TYPE MASTERSLICE ;
    PROPERTY LEF58_TYPE "TYPE BELOWDIEEDGE ; " ;
END BOTTOMDIE

Then, define the OBS layers in the macro for these constraints. The sample syntax for OBS layer
definition is given below:
……

OBS

      LAYER TOPDIE
      SPACING 10 ;
      RECT -10 -20 100.940 70.885 ;
      LAYER BOTTOMDIE
      SPACING 5 ;
      RECT 50 10 580 80.885 ;
      LAYER TOPPASSIV
      SPACING 20 ;
      RECT 200 -10 300 20 ;
      LAYER BACKPASSIV ;
      RECT 400 0 500 50 ;

To control the visibility of the OBS layers, click the Wire/Via tab of the Color Preferences form
(Options - All Colors) and select these layers.
To control the visibility of the OBS layers on BACKPASSIV and TOPPASSIV, click the Layer tab of
the Color Preferences form, and select these layers. To control the visibility of the OBS layers on
BOTTOMDIE and TOPDIE, click the All Colors button, select Custom tab, and then select these
layers.
OBS Layers Example




Last Updated in September 2025                            2104                                        Product Version 23.16
                                         Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
               Design Methodology for 3D IC with Through Silicon Via--Defining Keep Out Area in Hard Macros




Check Bump Keep Out Area Violation
Use the check_stacked_die -check_type {bump_keepout} command parameter to check the
overlap between the OBS layer and bumps (both front and back bumps). You can use
the check_stacked_die -check_type {die_edge} command parameter for die edge violation
checking between adjacent dies. Once you define an OBS layer inside a macro, the adjacent die
edge should not overlap this OBS area. If there is any violation between macro OBS and adjacent
die edge, it will be marked in the GUI window. The check_stacked_die command must be used
after read_die_abstract to input adjacent die’s die abstract file. Both top and bottom die edge
violation is checked at the same time.

3D IC Flow in Innovus
The following figure shows the general Innovus 3D IC design flow. Only 2 dies are included in this
flow chart, and it can be extended to multiple dies.
3D IC Design Flow in Innovus




Innovus designs one die at a time. When designing one die, the micro bump and the instance on
the adjacent die are honored and the micro bump on the current die is synchronized and optimized
accordingly. The design flow for each die is quite similar to the normal design, except the following
steps:
   1. In the design import stage, additional configuration for stacked die should be imported.
   2. In the floorplan stage, interface between each dies is ensured to be synchronized, and
      TSV/Bumps is created and assigned.
   3. After floorplanning, the TSV and bump should be routed.




Last Updated in September 2025                            2106                                        Product Version 23.16
                                       Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                       Design Methodology for 3D IC with Through Silicon Via--3D IC Flow in Innovus



   4. In the verification stage, the connectivity between dies should be verified.
The following figure shows the detailed floorplan flow for a 3D IC design.
Detailed Floorplan Flow for One of the Dies




Innovus provides TSV Tool Box to perform various 3D IC flow task. It contains the forms used in the
3D IC flow, including TSV/Bump manipulation, data exchange, TSV/Bump routing, and design
verification. Choose Tools -TSV to open it.
GUI Form of TSV Tool Box

Design Import and Export in 3D IC Designs

Import
Innovus takes the conventional and the additional information for stacked dies as inputs for each
die design.


Export
After design is finished, Innovus could export extra files for 3D IC designs except the conventional
files like DEF, GDSII etc. The extra files are used for downstream analysis tools for purposes of
STA, extraction, and rail analysis.
       For STA
       Innovus is able to create a top-level SPEF that describes the connectivity between dies. The
       RC is 0 in this SPEF file, but only inter-die connectivity is written. This SPEF file, together with
       the SPEF for each die, is the input of Tempus for static timing analysis. Use following
       commands:
       write_spef_between_tsv_dies

       For RC Extraction and Rail Analysis
       Innovus is able to write micro bump mapping file for Quantus and Voltus. It describes the
       connections of micro bumps between adjacent dies.
       write_micro_bump_mapping_file

       Design Methodology for 3D IC with Through Silicon Via

Stacked IC Verilog Input
Innovus inputs an additional top-level net list to describe the die-to-die interconnection and die to
package interconnection. In the top-level netlist, package is set as the top module; each die is
instantiated and the connectivity among dies is described. The following figure shows an example
of the top-level netlist.
Top-Level Netlist Example




The design .globals file needs to be modified to import the top-level netlist. Except for the module of
the die to be designed, the top-level netlist should also be included in the design .globals file.
Considering the above figure as an example, the following setting in the design configuration is for
Die1 design.
read_netlist { Die1.v top.v }

Stack Configuration Input
Innovus inputs an xml file that describes the position, flipping, and the orientation for each die
related to the package. The xml file could be loaded by the read_tsv_config command directly or
generated and edited through the Stacked Config Editor form.
<StackChip>

           <TopDesign name="top" offsetX=0.0 offsetY=0.0 orientation="R0" />

           <Tier number=1>

                <Chip name="Interposer" orientation="R0" faceUp="yes" llx=0 lly=0
sizeX=3740 sizeY=2340 scaleFactor=1 />

           </Tier>

           <Tier number=2>

                <Chip name="mother_die" orientation="R0" faceUp="no" llx=100 lly=100
sizeX=2140 sizeY=2140 scaleFactor=0.8 />

                <Chip name="daughter_die" orientation="R0" faceUp="no" llx=2300 lly=500
sizeX=1340 sizeY=1340 scaleFactor=1 />

           </Tier>

</StackChip>

Stack Config File Example

Power Connectivity Input
A text file is used to set up P/G net configuration on each die.
There are four types of P/G nets: frontside, backside, sharable, feedthru.
       frontside: The P/G net is connected to the front side bump only




Last Updated in September 2025                             2112                                         Product Version 23.16
                                        Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                      Design Methodology for 3D IC with Through Silicon Via--Power Connectivity Input



       backside: The P/G net is connected to the back side bump only
       sharable: The P/G net is connected to both front and back side bumps and other instance in
       the chip
       feedthru: The P/G net is connected to front and back side bumps directly, without the
       connection to any instance of current chip; use alias to define the same P/G net on different
       dies
The following figure shows an example of a power connectivity file.
Example of Power Configuration File

              Design Methodology for 3D IC with Through Silicon Via--Interface Synchronization and Information
                                                 Exchange between Dies




Interface Synchronization and Information
Exchange between Dies
In 3D ICs, the micro bump is the data/power path between dies. As shown in Figure 2, the pad of
micro bump should be exactly aligned to build the connection between the dies. Innovus uses the
bump file to transfer the bump information from one die to the adjacent die. The bump file contains
all the bump information of a die. Firstly, the bump file is dumped out
by write_bump_locations command from one die. Then read_bump_locations command should be
called on the adjacent die. The read_bump_locations command creates and assigns micro bumps
on the current die according to the bumps on the adjacent die, as shown in the following picture.


Figure 10 Interface Sync-Up Flow




The write/read bump information process could be iterated between the two adjacent dies. If the
micro bump on one of the dies is changed, the bump information should be written out and read by
the other die.
When designing one die, the adjacent die information should be honored and displayed. This step
is not essential, but helpful for optimization across the dies and for manual debug. Firstly, the die
abstract file is dumped out by the write_die_abstract command from one die. Then, the
read_die_abstract command should be called on the adjacent die.
The read_die_abstract command imports the adjacent die information. This information is



Last Updated in September 2025                              2114                                        Product Version 23.16
                                        Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                    Design Methodology for 3D IC with Through Silicon Via--TSV and Bump Manipulation



displayed and honored by the die being designed.


Related Information
       Design Methodology for 3D IC with Through Silicon Via
       Defining Keep Out Area in Hard Macros
       3D IC Flow in Innovus
       Design Import and Export in 3D IC Designs
       Stacked IC Verilog Input
       Stack Configuration Input
       Power Connectivity Input
       TSV and Bump Manipulation
       Feedthru Handling
       TSV and Bump Routing
       Cross Die Connectivity Verification


TSV and Bump Manipulation
Innovus provides the capability to create/delete and assign/unassign TSV and bumps.


TSV/Bump Generation
There are multiple ways to create TSV/Bumps in Innovus.
       Create TSV/Bumps according to bumps on adjacent die
       If there are some micro bumps fixed on adjacent dies, user could export the bump file for the
       adjacent die with write_bump_locations command. Then in the current die, import the bump
       file with read_bump_locations command and specify -front_bump, -back_bump, and
       –tsv_via_name option in read_bump_location to create corresponding front side bump, back
       side bump and TSV.
       Create TSV/Bumps on current die by command
       If there’s no micro bump fixed on adjacent dies, run add_tsv -, -front_bump, and -back_bump
       to create corresponding cells and specify -lower_left_location, -pitch_x_y, and



Last Updated in September 2025                            2115                                         Product Version 23.16
                                        Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                    Design Methodology for 3D IC with Through Silicon Via--TSV and Bump Manipulation



       -upper_right_location options to create expected TSV/Bump array.

Since TSVs and bumps are the path between the dies, place TSVs and back bumps close to the
IOs/Blocks whose pin connects to the adjacent dies on the back side. Keep TSVs outside the core
area, unless a TSV has to be connected with a block inside the core area because the TSVs will
break the follow pin.
TSV/Bumps in GUI




TSV/Bump Assignment
After creating TSV/Bump, user can now assign signal and PG TSV, frontside and backside bumps.
       Assign TSV/Bumps according to bumps on adjacent die
If there are some micro bumps fixed on adjacent dies, read_bump_locations help user create and
assign bumps automatically.
       Create TSV/Bumps on current die by command
Run assign_tsv to assigns nets to TSVs, front side and back side bumps, and/or feedthrus.
       assign_tsv -front_bump help user assign front side bumps with the IO pins.

       assign_tsv -back_bump help user assign back side bumps and TSVs.

       assign_tsv -tsv_via_name help user assign specified TSV cell only.




Last Updated in September 2025                            2116                                         Product Version 23.16
                                        Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                         Design Methodology for 3D IC with Through Silicon Via--Feedthru Handling



Related Information

       Design Methodology for 3D IC with Through Silicon Via

       Cross Die Connectivity Verification


Feedthru Handling
In a 3D IC design, there is a special kind of net called feedthrough net. The feedthrough net has
only two pins: one is the IO pin on the front side and the other is the IO pin on the back side. It does
not connect to any instance on the die. The feedthrough net on one die is defined for the connection
between the adjacent dies on the front side and back side, as shown in the following figure.
Logic Concept and Physical Components of Feedthrough




You can define the feedthrough net in the Verilog netlist by assigning the two IO pins on the front
side and back side together. To create a feedthrough, specify the -tsv_feedthru parameter of
the add_tsv command. The assign_tsv command assigns the feedthrough net and the normal net



Last Updated in September 2025                             2117                                     Product Version 23.16
                                        Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                       Design Methodology for 3D IC with Through Silicon Via--TSV and Bump Routing



simultaneously or separately by turning on and off the -feedthru and -non_feedthru parameters.
The embedded TSV, which is modeled as a pin on the back side metal can also be supported
by assign_tsv. The assign_tsv will not assign this back side pin to TSV.


Related Information

       Design Methodology for 3D IC with Through Silicon Via
       Defining Keep Out Area in Hard Macros
       3D IC Flow in Innovus
       Design Import and Export in 3D IC Designs
       Stacked IC Verilog Input
       Stack Configuration Input
       Power Connectivity Input
       Interface Synchronization and Information Exchange between Dies
       TSV and Bump Manipulation
       TSV and Bump Routing
       Cross Die Connectivity Verification


TSV and Bump Routing
In a 3D IC design, user usually needs to route TSV/Bump to bumps, PG stripes, instance pins, and
IO pads routing. Innovus provides several route engines to support routing for TSV/Bumps.




Last Updated in September 2025                            2118                                       Product Version 23.16
                                        Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                       Design Methodology for 3D IC with Through Silicon Via--TSV and Bump Routing




TSV to IO Pads/ Bumps/ PG Stripes Routing
   1. Route TSV to IO Pad. Use route_flip_chip with connect_bump_to_pad mode to support TSV
       to IO pad routing. For example:
       route_flip_chip -target connect_bump_to_pad -route_engine detail -
       connect_tsv_to_pad -route_width 3 -top_layer METAL4 -bottom_layer METAL1

   2. Route TSV to bump. Innovus cannot route front side bump and back side bump at the same
      time, which needs to route separately. For example, to route TSV and back side bump, set
      both top and bottom routing layer as back side layer and define extra configuration
      srouteExcludeBumpType. Use the following commands:


       route_flip_chip -target connect_bump_to_pad -route_engine detail -route_width 8 -
       top_layer MB -bottom_layer MB -connect_tsv_to_bump -extraConfig
       ./conf/backside.extraConf


       In the backside.extraConf, user needs to define "srouteExcludeBumpType FRONT_BUMP",
       "FRONT_BUMP" is the cell name of the front side bump.


       To route TSV and front side bump, set both top and bottom routing layer as front side layer
       and define extra configuration srouteExcludeBumpType:

       route_flip_chip -target connect_bump_to_pad -route_engine detail -route_width 8 -
       top_layer METAL4 -bottom_layer METAL1 -connect_tsv_to_bump -extra_config
       ./conf/frontside.extraConf


       In the frontside.extraConf, user need to define "srouteExcludeBumpType BACK_BUMP",
       "BACK_BUMP" is the cell name of the back side bump.

   3. Route TSV to power ground stripe. Use fcroute with target connect_bump_to_ring_stripe to
      route TSV and power groundstripes, for example:
       route_flip_chip -target connect_bump_to_ring_stripe -route_width 6 -
       top_layer METAL2 -bottom_layer METAL1


       The embedded TSV is modeled as a pin on back side metal. It is supported
       by route_flip_chip, which will honor the pin on back side metal.




Last Updated in September 2025                            2119                                       Product Version 23.16
                                        Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                       Design Methodology for 3D IC with Through Silicon Via--TSV and Bump Routing



TSV/Bumps Routing




Bump to Bump Routing

Front Bump to Front Bump Routing

An interposer design has no instance in the design and requires front bump connect to front bump
directly. You can use nano route to route them. Use the following setting and commands:
set_db route_design_selected_net_only true

set_db route_design_connect_to_bumps true

route_design

Bump to Bump Routing




Last Updated in September 2025                            2120                                       Product Version 23.16
                                        Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                       Design Methodology for 3D IC with Through Silicon Via--TSV and Bump Routing




Front Bump to Front Bump Bus Routing
In interposer design that connects HBM dies and SoC dies, usually bus routing is required to
connect HBM dies and SoC dies to ensure the signal transmission performance. This section
illustrates the routing methodology for bus routing in interposer design.
Routing Steps:
   1. Create Non-Default Rule (NDR) and Bus Routing Constraints.
          a. Following command defines the NDR (2 um width and 3 um spacing on layer METAL2
             and METAL4) to be used on bus routing:
              create_route_rule -width {METAL2 2.0 METAL4 2.0} -spacing {METAL2 3 METAL2 3}
              -name bus_ndr

          b. Following command defines bus net constraints to be used in routing. It requires tool
             using only METAL2 as bus routing layer and bus_ndr as routing rule. Tool may switch
             layers of last few wire segments when connecting to bump pins.
              set_integration_route_constraint -type bus -top_layer METAL2 -bottom_layer
              METAL2 -rule bus_ndr -net {bus_net_1 bus_net_2 …}

   2. Set interlayer shielding constraints
      In some scenarios, user may want to create shielding wires between signal routing layers. It is
      named as “interlayer shielding”.
          a. Interlayer Shielding offset
             Following example defines the offset (2.8 um) between centerline of signal wires and



Last Updated in September 2025                            2121                                       Product Version 23.16
                                        Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                       Design Methodology for 3D IC with Through Silicon Via--TSV and Bump Routing



              centerline of interlayer shielding wires on METAL1 and METAL3 layer.
              set_db route_design_interposer_interlayer_shielding_offsets {METAL1 2.8
              METAL3 2.8}

          b. Interlayer Shielding Width
             Following example defines routing width (2 um) of interlayer shielding wires on layer
             METAL1 and METAL3.
              set_db route_design_interposer_interlayer_shielding_widths {METAL1 2.0 METAL3
              2.0}

          c. Interlayer shielding layers
             Following example defines interlayer shielding wires are routed below M2 and M4 layer,
             respectively.
              set_db route_design_interposer_interlayer_shielding_layers {METAL2 bottom
              METAL4 bottom}

          d. Interlayer shielding net name
             Following example defines shielding net name is VSS for shielding wires in the layer
             range of METAL1 to METAL4
              set_db route_design_interposer_interlayer_shielding_nets {METAL1:METAL4 VSS}

   3. Set same-layer interleaved shielding constraints
      In some scenarios, user may want to create shielding wires between signal routing wires on
      same layer. It is named as “interleaved shielding”.
          a. set_db flip_chip_constraint_file fc_shield.const

          b. fc_shield.const file content.
             Following example defines shielding width to be 1.0 um, shielding-to-signal spacing to
             be 2.0 um and shielding net is VSS
              SHIELDWIDTH 1.0
              SHIELDGAP 2.0
              SHIELDSTYLE c
              SHIELDNET VSS

   4. Create PG stripes over PG bumps
   5. Route constrained Bus nets and shielding
       route_design -bump

Shielding Pattern




Last Updated in September 2025                            2122                                       Product Version 23.16
                                        Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                       Design Methodology for 3D IC with Through Silicon Via--TSV and Bump Routing




Front Bump to TSV-Bump Feedthru Net Routing

In some situations, TSV via and back side bump are packed together and formed a new bump cell.
It is named TSV bump. TSV bump usually has multiple geometries on both front-side, TSV layer
and back-side. route_flip_chip has the capability to route from front bump to TSV-bumps by using
option -connect_tsv_to_bump and constraint BUMP_AS_PAD.
route_flip_chip -target connect_bump_to_pad -connect_tsv_to_bump -top_layer METAL4 -
bottom_layer METAL1 -constraint_file fc.const -nets “net1 net2 …”

In the flipchip.const, user need to define "BUMP_AS_PAD" constraint, "TSV_bump_cell_name" is
the cell name of TSV-bumps.
BUMP_AS_PAD

<TSV_bump_cell_names>

END BUMP_AS_PAD

TSV-Bump cell




Last Updated in September 2025                            2123                                       Product Version 23.16
                                        Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                       Design Methodology for 3D IC with Through Silicon Via--TSV and Bump Routing




TSV/Bump to Instance Pin Routing
In 3D stacked die design, sometimes there is no IO pads. TSVs and front bumps connect to
instance pins directly. Innovus supports it by using nano route. Use the following setting and
commands:
set_db route_design_selected_net_only true

set_db route_design_connect_to_bumps true

route_design

Cross Die Connectivity Verification
The check_connectivity command checks whether the connectivity between the dies is correctly
implemented, that is, the micro bumps on the adjacent die with the same signal are aligned.
To check the micro bump alignment on one die, you need to dump the die abstract of all the
adjacent dies, and then run the command check_connectivity -die_abstract_file. The violation
will be shown on the violation browser, and the violation marker will be displayed on the layout
window.
read_bump_locations -check_alignment can also be used for checking bump alignment.

                  Safety Implementation and Verification with USF--Introduction to Functional Safety Analysis




Introduction to Functional Safety Analysis
Functional safety is important for automotive and aerospace industries. The focus is on ensuring:
       Reliability by minimizing failures during product life by taking steps to prevent, detect, or
       correct failures.
       Safety by reducing the risk of injury in the event of a system failure
The international standard for functional safety of electrical and electronic automotive systems is
ISO 26262. The ISO 26262 certification uses Automotive Safety Integrity Level (ASIL) to measure
and classify risk. ASIL takes into account the probability of failure, controllability by driver, and
severity of failure to assess events and classify safety goals on an A-B-C-D scale, with A being the
least stringent and D being the most stringent.




Related Information




Last Updated in September 2025                               2127                                          Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                  Safety Implementation and Verification with USF--Introduction to Functional Safety Analysis




     Safety Versus Reliability

     Midas Safety Platform

     Safety Mechanisms

     Unified Safety Format (USF) File


Safety Versus Reliability
Reliability focuses on minimizing failures during a product's life.
Cadence provides integrated solutions across Innovus, Voltus, Tempus, and Pegasus to improve
reliability:
       100% Multi-Cut Insertion and ViaPillars as via failures are major source of yield loss.




       Reducing electromigration (EM) through NDR widening, timing-aware driver downsizing, and
       load split with timing-aware buffering.




Last Updated in September 2025                               2128                                          Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                  Safety Implementation and Verification with USF--Introduction to Functional Safety Analysis



       Reducing IR-Drop
              IR-aware placement that spreads high power density hotspots
              Power grid stripe addition in hotspots




              Timing-aware IR-drop fixing flow with Tempus-ECO
       Handling aging as cell delays increase with device aging.
              Aging-aware STA with Cadence® Liberate™ plus Tempus™ solution




              Accurate analysis leads to improvements in PPA
In contrast to reliability, safety focuses on preventing injury in the event of a failure. The subsequent
sections within Safety Implementation and Verification with USF focus on safety.




Last Updated in September 2025                               2129                                          Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                  Safety Implementation and Verification with USF--Introduction to Functional Safety Analysis




Midas Safety Platform
The Cadence Midas™ Safety Platform analyzes the functional safety of a system using Failure
Mode Effect and Diagnostic Analysis (FMEDA). It numerically estimates how a system can fail and
report on ISO 26262 metrics using ASIL. Midas also assists you in adding safety mechanisms to
achieve the target ASIL by inserting extra logic to detect or correct faults. It captures safety intent in
a Unified Safety Format (USF) file.
The inputs required by Midas are:
       Design information in the form of an RTL or a netlist.
       Fault simulation results from Jasper or Xcelium.
       Failure rate for the standard cells, memories, and so on in terms of the number of expected
       failures in time (FIT). FIT is the number of failures that can be expected in one billion device-
       hours of operation (~114,000 years) of a component.
       Safety mechanisms implemented to protect failure modes and improve a design's safety
       metrics by bypassing, detecting, or correcting faults.
       Diagnostic coverage, which measures the effectiveness of a safety mechanism at detecting
       faults and bringing the system into a safe state.




Last Updated in September 2025                               2130                                          Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                  Safety Implementation and Verification with USF--Introduction to Functional Safety Analysis




Safety Mechanisms
A Failure Mode (FM) is a part of your design that has the potential to fail, such as an inst, hinst, or
group. Safety mechanisms protect failure modes and improve a design's safety metrics by
bypassing, detecting, or correcting faults. They improve functional safety by increasing the
diagnostic coverage, which is the ability to detect a failure and bring the system into a safe state.
Safety mechanisms are physically implemented by adding extra hardware to achieve redundancy
of functionality. To prevent common cause failures from breaking a safety mechanism, its original
and redundant logic must be independent and physically separate.
Innovus supports the following safety mechanisms:
       Soft Error Resilience (SER)
       Dual Core Lock Step (DCLS)
       Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR)
       Parity


Soft Error Resilience (SER)
Soft errors are temporary errors in the data being processed. These are random in nature and do
not cause any actual damage to the system’s hardware.
Soft errors are caused due to radiation particle strikes, which can induce a charge on a transistor,
flipping its logic value.
       Sequential logic: Soft errors on registers can produce functional errors.
       Combinational logic: Change in combinational logic value can cause functional errors if
       propagated to, and stored in, a sequential element. Soft errors thus increase with clock
       frequency.
Whether or not a radiation particle strike changes the logic value depends on the energy level of the
particle and the critical charge of the device. Critical charge is the minimum charge required to flip a
device. It is dependent on the transistor capacitance and the operating voltage. This means soft
errors increase as technologies shrink.
Soft errors also increase with altitude as radiation levels increase.
Some library vendors provide radiation-hardened flip-flops, which are known to be more resilient
against soft error events as compared to the normal flip-flops in the standard cell library. The Soft
Error Resilience (SER) flow is intended for applications with these radiation-hardened flip-flop cells,




Last Updated in September 2025                               2131                                          Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                  Safety Implementation and Verification with USF--Introduction to Functional Safety Analysis



also called ser cells.




Ser cells are more resilient due to radiation hardening and built-in redundancy. However, ser cells
are larger and slower than normal flip-flops and consume more power. Therefore, their PPA cost is
higher.


Dual Core Lock Step (DCLS)
A Dual Core Lock Step (DCLS) safety mechanism usually consists of two or three modules. For
example, it can comprise:
       A safety critical primary module
       A redundant clone module that performs the same task as the primary module
       A compare module that ensures that the other two modules are in sync and that an error has
       not occurred
For the safety mechanism to be effective, the DCLS modules or groups must be physically
separated. This is to prevent a common fault from impacting more than one of the modules, which
would break the safety mechanism.




Last Updated in September 2025                               2132                                          Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                  Safety Implementation and Verification with USF--Introduction to Functional Safety Analysis




Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR)

In a Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) system:
       For safety critical parent flops, two clone flops and voting logic will be added.
       The value stored on at least two of the three flops will be propagated downstream.
       If an error occurs on a single flop, the correct value will still be propagated.
For the safety mechanism to be effective, the three flops must be physically separated. This is to
prevent a common fault from impacting more than one of the flops, which would break the safety
mechanism.




Parity
A Parity safety mechanism is defined for a group of design flops that require checking. To
implement this mechanism:
       Add new logic to generate a parity bit based on the values driving those flops.
       Add a new flop to store the parity-bit value.
       Add new logic to check the parity-bit value against the value stored on the design flops.
If any of the design flops is flipped, an error signal is generated.




Last Updated in September 2025                               2133                                          Product Version 23.16
                                          Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
                  Safety Implementation and Verification with USF--Introduction to Functional Safety Analysis



Related Information

       Implementing SER
       Implementing DCLS
       Implementing TMR
       Implementing Parity


Unified Safety Format (USF) File
Safety intent standardization is important to support data exchange and traceability across different
safety analysis and work products.
A Unified Safety Format (USF) file captures the functional safety intent of a semiconductor design,
in a similar way to how a Unified Power Format (UPF) file captures the power intent of a design. A
USF file includes the information required for the specification, implementation, verification,
modeling, and analysis of safety critical systems and safety-critical electronic intellectual property
(IP).
USF enables the portability of functional safety intent across a variety of tools throughout an
electronic system design verification and implementation flow. It also facilitates the automation of
analysis, design, verification, and implementation tools that must account for the safety critical
aspects of a design.
For example, Midas™ can be used to define safety intent, Genus™ and Innovus™ can be used for
automatic insertion and implementation of safety mechanisms, while Conformal® can be used for
formal verification of safety mechanisms.




Innovus supports the Physical configuration of Midas USF. To enable USF in Innovus, you must set
the safety_midas_enable attribute to physical.
set_db safety_midas_enable physical

You will see a message like this:




Last Updated in September 2025                               2134                                          Product Version 23.16
                                         Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
               Safety Implementation and Verification with USF--Implementing Safety Mechanisms with Innovus

Cadence and the Cadence logo are registered trademarks of Cadence Design Systems Inc.
in the United States and other countries.
Midas USF - Physical Version: 23.09.004
1 physical

The Midas USF Physical configuration implements the commands targeting automatic safety
mechanism generation and design implementation.
For more details on these commands, see Unified Safety Format Commands and Attributes in
the Innovus Stylus Common UI Text Command Reference.

       Innovus_Automotive_Opt (INVS56): This license is required for any implementation feature
       enabled by the presence of a USF file. For example:
              dcls: placement, routing, clock isolation
              tmr: cloning, placement, clock isolation

Implementing SER
Soft errors are temporary errors caused due to radiation particle strikes that may induce a charge on
a transistor, flipping its logic value. These errors are random in nature and do not cause any actual
damage to the system’s hardware.
Soft errors increase with clock frequency and altitude. They also increase as technologies shrink.
Some library vendors provide radiation-hardened flip-flops, which are known to be more resilient
against soft error events as compared to the normal flip-flops in the standard cell library. The Soft
Error Resilience (SER) flow is intended for applications with these radiation-hardened flip-flop cells,
also called ser cells.




Ser cells are more resilient due to radiation hardening and built-in redundancy. However, ser cells
are larger and slower than normal flip-flops and consume more power. Therefore, their PPA cost is



Last Updated in September 2025                            2136                                        Product Version 23.16
                                         Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
               Safety Implementation and Verification with USF--Implementing Safety Mechanisms with Innovus



higher.
Innovus supports the USF flow to map safety critical registers to ser cells. It supports both single-bit
and multi-bit flops.
You can define the SER safety mechanism rules by using the
set_safety_mechanism_rules_ser USF command:

set_safety_mechanism_rules_ser \

  -cells {<cells>}                                                             #For designs without power
domains.

  -cells { {{PD1} {<cells>}} {{PD2 PD3} {<cells>}} }                           #For designs with power
domains.

To see related violations, check the following name in the check_safety_mechanism report:
   SER.CEL: flop-bits assigned ser but without an ser-cell

Optionally, you can specify the SER type as hard or soft. A setting of soft allows a trade-off
between timing and SER cell selection.


Related Information

       check_safety_mechanism

       set_safety_mechanism_rules_ser

Implementing TMR
In a Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) system:
       For safety critical parent flops, two clone flops and voting logic will be added.
       The value stored on at least two of the three flops will be propagated downstream.
       If an error occurs on a single flop, the correct value will still be propagated.
For the safety mechanism to be effective, the three flops must be physically separated. This is to




Last Updated in September 2025                            2138                                        Product Version 23.16
                                         Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
               Safety Implementation and Verification with USF--Implementing Safety Mechanisms with Innovus



prevent a common fault from impacting more than one of the flops, which would break the safety
mechanism.
       TMR Insertion
       Placement Separation
       Clock Isolation
       Verification
       Reading the TMR Information File
       USF Physical File for TMR
       Checking the TMR Implementation


TMR Insertion
TMR insertion occurs during synthesis in Genus. For safety-critical parent flops, two clone flops and
voting logic are added. Genus supports custom voter module or cell.
If not specified, Genus will automatically create voting logic.




To see related violations, check the following name in the check_safety_mechanism report:
   TMR.GRP: flops assigned tmr but without a tmr group


Placement Separation
Maintain the required spacing between the three flops in a TMR group throughout the flow. Use the
set_safety_mechanism_rules_tmr USF command to specify spacing. Separation can be either:

       x/y-based: Specified using the -spacing_x and/or -spacing_y



Last Updated in September 2025                            2139                                        Product Version 23.16
                                         Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
               Safety Implementation and Verification with USF--Implementing Safety Mechanisms with Innovus


       x/y-based: Specified using the -spacing_x and/or -spacing_y
       or
       Radial: Specified using the -spacing parameter.




To see related violations, check the following name in the check_safety_mechanism report:
   TMR.SPA: spacing



Related Information

       check_safety_mechanism

       set_safety_mechanism_rules_tmr




Last Updated in September 2025                            2140                                        Product Version 23.16
                                         Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
               Safety Implementation and Verification with USF--Implementing Safety Mechanisms with Innovus




Clock Isolation
You can isolate the signals driving TMR flops by using clock, reset, or scan_enable isolation.




split_net Isolation

In this type of TMR isolation, the final driver/net directly driving a TMR parent/clone flop cannot
directly drive another parent/clone flop from the same TMR group.
split_net isolation is supported for:
       Clock




Last Updated in September 2025                            2141                                        Product Version 23.16
                                         Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
               Safety Implementation and Verification with USF--Implementing Safety Mechanisms with Innovus




You can implement split_net isloation in the TMR flow using the following command:
set_safety_mechanism_rules_tmr \
  -isolate_clock split_net ;


To see related violations, check the following name in the check_safety_mechanism report:
    TMR.ISO.CLK.SN: isolation - clock - split_net



split_network Isolation

In this type of TMR isolation, each network branch can drive:
       TMR and non-TMR flops.
       Different TMR groups.
       Only one of the three flops in each TMR group.
split_network isolation is supported for:
       Clock



Last Updated in September 2025                            2142                                        Product Version 23.16
                                         Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
               Safety Implementation and Verification with USF--Implementing Safety Mechanisms with Innovus



       Reset
       Scan_enable




You can implement split_network isloation in the TMR flow using the following command:
set_safety_mechanism_rules_tmr \
  -isolate_clock split_network \
  -isolate_reset split_network \
  -isolate_scan_enable split_network ;

To see related violations, check the following name in the check_safety_mechanism report:
         TMR.ISO.CLK.SNW: isolation - clock - split_network
         TMR.ISO.RST.SNW: isolation - reset - split_network
         TMR.ISO.SE.SNW : isolation - scan_enable - split_network


Verification
Verification involves:
       Equivalence checking: Compare the pre-TMR netlist with the post-TMR netlist for




Last Updated in September 2025                            2143                                        Product Version 23.16
                                         Innovus Stylus Common UI User Guide
               Safety Implementation and Verification with USF--Implementing Safety Mechanisms with Innovus



       equivalence.
       TMR logic validation: Validate the inserted TMR logic (clone flops and voting logic) for correct
       functionality.




Reading the TMR Information File
Use the read_safety_tmr command to read the TMR information file. For designs with TMR logic
already inserted, either in the register-transfer level (RTL) or with third party tools, this command
identifies the existing TMR logic and assimilates it into the Unified Safety Format (USF).
You can issue the read_safety_tmr command multiple times to build up information in cases where
there are multiple different safety mechanism names or failure mode names. However, this may
be done only for new safety mechanism and failure mode names.


Related Information

       read_safety_tmr

For details on usage, see read_safety_tmr in the Innovus Text Command Reference.
